blocks.markdown:
The website wears one design system across every surface — the docs, the landing, the specimen. The system is two things: a vocabulary of design tokens, and two themes that give those tokens values. A theme is a class on the app root, `loom-theme-light` or `loom-theme-dark`, and everything downstream reads the tokens rather than raw colours. Switching theme swaps one class; nothing else moves.

The type is literate before it is anything else. Reading matter is set in a serif — IBM Plex Serif — because long prose reads better with serifs. The interface around it — the top bar, the sidebar, the outline — is set in a sans, IBM Plex Sans, so the chrome stays quiet and distinct from the text. Code is IBM Plex Mono. Three faces from one family, each with a clear job.

The app root carries the type scale and the layout measures, and reads its colours from whichever theme class sits beside it. The measure caps the reading column near seventy characters; collapsing a sidebar widens it. Oldstyle numerals and ligatures are on, because the page is a document.

The two themes give the vocabulary its values. The light theme is Gruvbox Light Soft — a warm sand page, dark-brown text, a terracotta accent — softer on the eye than a paper white. The dark theme is the deep editor slate this project already used: the same page, surface, and code backgrounds as the landing, with a calm blue accent that carries the wordmark and every active mark. Each theme sets the whole vocabulary, so no surface has to reach outside its theme for a colour.

The top bar spans the app: the wordmark and section links on the left, the search trigger and the theme switch on the right. It is set in the sans, so it reads as chrome. The wordmark's mark is a serif italic tilde in the accent colour — the one place the brand shows. The theme switch is a pill with a knob that slides and recolours as the theme flips.

blocks.title: The shell and the left sidebar

blocks.markdown: Below the top bar, three columns hold the page: the sidebar of parts and chapters, the reading column, and the outline. Collapsing either side rebalances the grid. The sidebar lists each part and its chapters; the active chapter carries an accent rail down its left edge, and its own headings unfold beneath it as a dotted sublist.

The reading column is the document. It centres on the measure and sets everything in the serif: a large title, an italic deck beneath it, a sans meta line, then the prose. Each block type — a heading, a paragraph, a list, a code block with its language and path, a callout, a blockquote, a table, and the prev/next pager — has a settled shape. Code anchors keep the accent underline that links carry, so a reference reads as live.

The outline is the right rail: the current page's headings, under an "on this page" label, each with the same accent rail the sidebar uses to mark where you are. Below them sit the page's own links — the source and the book.

Two overlays float above the page. The search palette — the telescope — opens on a dim, blurred scrim: a serif italic title, a sans input, results grouped by kind with a mono path each, and a footer of key hints. The shortcuts card is quieter and smaller, a plain list of what each key does.
The scrollbars stay out of the way — thin, and only as dark as a strong border.
The landing has its own layout — a hero that pairs the headline with a real loom, a row of three claims, and a footer. It reads the same tokens as everything else, so it turns with the theme.

The stylesheet is the type and tokens, the two themes, and then each surface that reads them: the top bar, the shell and sidebar, the reading column, the outline, the two overlays, the landing, and the quiet scrollbars.

blocks.markdown: The landing is the front page — the `/` route. It says what Loom is in one line, shows a real loom beside it, and points to the two ways in: read the book, or read the source. It is one exported view, `landingView`, that the reading app frames with the top bar. It is deliberately small: a hero, three plain claims, a footer. No chrome it does not need.

The landing borrows the whole of its runtime from the model — the view builder `h`, the two helpers that address the book, and the `Copy` message that carries the install line to the clipboard. It builds `Html`, the same node type every view in the app returns.

blocks.code:
import { Copy, firstSlug, h, pathForSlug } from './model'
import type { Html } from '@athrio/foldkit/html'

blocks.markdown: The hero shows a real loom — the greeting from the first chapter, exactly as it is written. It is the shortest thing that shows the shape: frontmatter that names the language and the file, a `# heading` that names a section, `=>` that turns prose into code, a `{Tangle}` section that composes the rest by anchor. A reader sees the marks before they read a word about them.

blocks.code:
const example = [
  '---',
  'Language: TypeScript',
  'Package: src/main.ts',
  '---',
  '',
  '# A greeting',
  '',
  'The greet function welcomes someone by name.',
  '',
  '=>',
  '',
  'export const greet = (name: string): string =>',
  '  `Hello, ${name}!`',
  '',
  '# The entry point {Tangle}',
  '',
  '=>',
  '',
  '::' + '[A greeting]',
  '',
  'console.log(greet("world"))',
].join('\n')

blocks.markdown: The hero is two halves. On the left, the line — “prose and code, woven into one source” — the lede beneath it, the install command with a copy button, and the two ways in. On the right, the loom. The install button dispatches `Copy`, so the one browser effect stays a message the app turns into a command.
Three claims say what makes a loom different — woven, tangled, alive in the editor — and a thin footer closes the page. `featureView` is the one repeated shape.

blocks.code:
const featureView = (title: string, body: string): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-feature')],
    [
      h.div([h.Class('loom-feature-title')], [title]),
      h.div([h.Class('loom-feature-body')], [body]),
    ],
  )

const featuresView = (): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-features')],
    [
      featureView(
        'Woven, not commented',
        'Prose and code are equal layers. A section draws in another by name — never by copying it.',
      ),
      featureView(
        'Tangled to real source',
        'loom tangle drops the prose and writes plain TypeScript, Bash, or JSON — the file the compiler sees.',
      ),
      featureView(
        'Alive in the editor',
        'Every section is first-class: go to an anchor’s definition, catch a broken one, type-check in place.',
      ),
    ],
  )

const footView = (): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-foot')],
    [
      h.span([], ['© 2026 Loom']),
      h.span(
        [],
        [h.a([h.Href(pathForSlug(firstSlug))], ['Docs']), ' · ', h.a([h.Href('#')], ['Source'])],
      ),
    ],
  )

blocks.markdown: `landingView` is the scrolling page: the hero, the claims, and the footer, centred on one column.

blocks.code:

export const landingView = (): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-landing')],

blocks.markdown:
The page is the shell the app mounts into. It holds an empty root, loads the built module, and gives the document its height. Nothing else lives here: the stylesheet brings its own fonts through an `@import`, and the app draws everything below `body`.

The one rule the shell must set is height. The app is a full-height flex column — a top bar over a scrolling body — so the root, the body, and the document all stretch to the window. Without that, a docs page has no height for its columns to fill.

The reading app is the woven book, running. It is a Foldkit application — the Elm Architecture on Effect: the shared `Model`, one `update`, and a `view` that is a pure function of the model. The view branches on the route: the landing fronts the site, and a chapter lays the book out in three columns — a sidebar of parts and chapters, the reading column, and the outline of the page. The book drives everything; the app lays it out.

The model module supplies the state, the messages, the routing, and the woven book itself. The landing module supplies its own view, which this app frames but does not build. From `effect` come the array, option, and match combinators; from `@athrio/foldkit` the runtime, the navigation, and the command that carries an effect. `marked` renders a prose block's Markdown to HTML.

blocks.code:
import { Array, Effect, Match, Option, pipe, Schema as S } from 'effect'
import { Command, Navigation, Runtime, Url } from '@athrio/foldkit'
import { type Document, type Html } from '@athrio/foldkit/html'
import type { WovenBlock, WovenNavEntry, WovenPage, WovenPart } from '@athrio/loom-lang/weave/WovenCorpus'
import { marked } from 'marked'
import {
  ChangedUrl,
  ClickedLink,
  Copy,
  CompletedNavigation,
  ToggledNav,
  ToggledTheme,
  ToggledToc,
  Model,
  firstSlug,
  h,
  pathForSlug,
  routeForPath,
  site,
  type Message,
} from './model'
import { landingView } from './landing'

blocks.markdown: `update` is the state machine, driven through the URL. A clicked link issues a navigation command rather than changing state directly: `NavigateInternal` pushes a chapter's path onto history, `NavigateExternal` leaves for another site, and the address change returns as `ChangedUrl`, which sets the route. So back, forward, deep links, and refresh all work. `CopyToClipboard` carries the one browser effect the landing needs. The toggles flip their flags.

Each block renders to a view. A heading becomes an `h1`–`h3` carrying the fragment id an anchor targets, styled by its level. Prose renders its Markdown to HTML with `marked`, so the reading-column rules dress the paragraphs inside. A code section is split at its anchors: the text between them stays plain, and each `::[…]` becomes an accent link that opens the section it composes. `codeSegments` walks the anchors in order, slicing the code around each.

blocks.code:
const headingClass = (level: number): string =>
  Match.value(level).pipe(
    Match.when(1, () => 'loom-h1'),
    Match.when(2, () => 'loom-h2'),
    Match.orElse(() => 'loom-h3'),
  )

const headingTag = (level: number) =>
  Match.value(level).pipe(
    Match.when(1, () => h.h1),
    Match.when(2, () => h.h2),
    Match.orElse(() => h.h3),
  )

const headingView = (block: Extract<WovenBlock, { type: 'heading' }>): Html =>
  headingTag(block.level)([h.Id(block.id), h.Class(headingClass(block.level))], [block.title])

const proseView = (block: Extract<WovenBlock, { type: 'prose' }>): Html =>
  h.div([h.InnerHTML(marked.parse(block.markdown) as string)], [])

const codeSegments = (
  block: Extract<WovenBlock, { type: 'code' }>,
): ReadonlyArray<string | Html> => {
  const walked = Array.reduce(
    block.links,
    { nodes: [] as ReadonlyArray<string | Html>, cursor: 0 },
    (acc, link) => ({
      nodes: [
        ...acc.nodes,
        block.code.slice(acc.cursor, link.offset),
        h.a(
          [h.Class('loom-anchor'), h.Href(pathForSlug(link.targetSlug))],
          [block.code.slice(link.offset, link.offset + link.length)],
        ),
      ],
      cursor: link.offset + link.length,
    }),
  )
  return [...walked.nodes, block.code.slice(walked.cursor)]
}

const codeView = (block: Extract<WovenBlock, { type: 'code' }>): Html =>
  h.div([h.Class('loom-code')], [h.pre([], [h.code([], codeSegments(block))])])

const noteView = (block: Extract<WovenBlock, { type: 'note' }>): Html =>
  h.aside(
    [h.Class('loom-callout')],
    [
      h.div([h.Class('loom-callout-label')], ['Note']),
      h.div([h.InnerHTML(marked.parse(block.markdown) as string)], []),
    ],
  )

const blockView = (block: WovenBlock): Html =>
  Match.value(block).pipe(
    Match.when({ type: 'heading' }, headingView),
    Match.when({ type: 'prose' }, proseView),
    Match.when({ type: 'code' }, codeView),
    Match.when({ type: 'note' }, noteView),
    Match.exhaustive,
  )

blocks.title: The page, the sidebar, and the outline

blocks.markdown: `pageOf` finds a chapter by slug, and `docsPage` falls back to the first. `headingsOf` pulls a page's section headings — the `h2` and `h3` blocks — which the sidebar's active sublist and the outline both read to show where you are within a chapter. The sidebar lists every part and its chapters, the current one carrying the accent rail; the outline repeats its headings under an "on this page" label.

blocks.code:
type Heading = { readonly id: string; readonly level: number; readonly title: string }

const pageOf = (slug: string): Option.Option<WovenPage> =>
  Array.findFirst(site.pages, (page) => page.slug === slug)

const docsPage = (slug: string): WovenPage => Option.getOrElse(pageOf(slug), () => site.pages[0])

const headingsOf = (page: WovenPage): ReadonlyArray<Heading> =>
  pipe(
    page.blocks,
    Array.map((block) =>
      block.type === 'heading' && block.level >= 2
        ? Option.some<Heading>({ id: block.id, level: block.level, title: block.title })
        : Option.none<Heading>(),
    ),
    Array.getSomes,
  )

const subHeadingView = (heading: Heading): Html =>
  h.li(
    [h.Key(heading.id)],
    [h.a([h.Href(`#${heading.id}`)], [h.span([h.Class('loom-sublist-dot')], []), heading.title])],
  )

const chapterView = (current: string) => (chapter: WovenNavEntry): Html => {
  const active = chapter.slug === current
  return h.li(
    active ? [h.Key(chapter.slug), h.Class('is-active')] : [h.Key(chapter.slug)],
    [
      h.a(
        [h.Class('loom-sidebar-item'), h.Href(pathForSlug(chapter.slug))],
        [
          h.span([h.Class('loom-sidebar-rail')], []),
          h.span([h.Class('loom-sidebar-num')], [chapter.number]),
          h.span([], [chapter.title]),
        ],
      ),
      ...(active
        ? [
            h.ul(
              [h.Class('loom-sidebar-sublist')],
              Array.map(
                pipe(pageOf(chapter.slug), Option.map(headingsOf), Option.getOrElse(() => [] as ReadonlyArray<Heading>)),
                subHeadingView,
              ),
            ),
          ]
        : []),
    ],
  )
}

const partView = (current: string) => (part: WovenPart): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-sidebar-section'), h.Key(part.number)],
    [
      h.div([h.Class('loom-sidebar-heading')], [part.name]),
      ...(part.chapters.length > 0
        ? [h.ul([h.Class('loom-sidebar-list')], Array.map(part.chapters, chapterView(current)))]
        : []),
    ],
  )

const sidebarView = (current: string): Html =>
  h.aside(
    [h.Class('loom-sidebar')],
    [
      h.div(
        [h.Class('loom-sidebar-inner')],
        [
          ...Array.map(site.nav, partView(current)),
          h.div([h.Class('loom-sidebar-footer')], [h.div([h.Class('loom-sidebar-kicker')], ['loom · v0.9.0'])]),
        ],
      ),
    ],
  )

const outlineEntryView = (heading: Heading): Html =>
  h.li(
    heading.level >= 3 ? [h.Key(heading.id), h.Class('loom-toc-sub')] : [h.Key(heading.id)],
    [h.a([h.Href(`#${heading.id}`)], [h.span([h.Class('loom-toc-rail')], []), heading.title])],
  )

const outlineView = (page: WovenPage): Html =>
  h.aside(
    [h.Class('loom-toc')],
    [
      h.div([h.Class('loom-toc-heading')], ['On this page']),
      h.ul([h.Class('loom-toc-list')], Array.map(headingsOf(page), outlineEntryView)),
      h.div([h.Class('loom-toc-footer')], [h.a([h.Href('/')], ['Home']), h.a([h.Href('/')], ['Source'])]),
    ],
  )

blocks.title: The top bar and the frame

blocks.markdown: The top bar spans the app: the sidebar toggle and the wordmark on the left, the section link in the middle, the theme switch on the right — the sidebar toggle only where there is a sidebar to toggle. `appFrame` wraps the top bar over whatever content the route calls for, on a root that carries the theme class. The reading column centres the current chapter on the measure: a breadcrumb of part and title, then the chapter's blocks.

blocks.code:
const topBarView = (model: Model): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class('loom-topbar')],
    [
      h.div(
        [h.Class('loom-topbar-left')],
        [
          ...(model.route._tag === 'Docs'
            ? [h.button([h.Class('loom-icon-btn'), h.OnClick(ToggledNav())], ['☰'])]
            : []),
          h.a(
            [h.Class('loom-wordmark'), h.Href('/')],
            [
              h.span([h.Class('loom-wordmark-mark')], ['~']),
              h.span([h.Class('loom-wordmark-text')], ['loom']),
              h.span([h.Class('loom-wordmark-ver')], ['v0.9.0']),
            ],
          ),
        ],
      ),
      h.nav(
        [h.Class('loom-topnav')],
        [
          h.a(
            [
              h.Class(model.route._tag === 'Docs' ? 'loom-topnav-item loom-topnav-active' : 'loom-topnav-item'),
              h.Href(pathForSlug(firstSlug)),
            ],
            ['Docs'],
          ),
        ],
      ),
      h.div(
        [h.Class('loom-topbar-right')],
        [
          h.button(
            [h.Class('loom-theme-toggle'), h.OnClick(ToggledTheme())],
            [
              h.span([h.Class(model.theme === 'dark' ? 'loom-theme-knob is-dark' : 'loom-theme-knob')], []),
              h.span([], [model.theme === 'dark' ? 'Dark' : 'Light']),
            ],
          ),
        ],
      ),
    ],
  )

const appFrame = (model: Model, content: Html): Html =>
  h.div([h.Class(`loom-app loom-theme-${model.theme}`)], [topBarView(model), content])

const mainView = (model: Model, page: WovenPage): Html =>
  h.div(
    [h.Class(model.navClosed ? 'loom-main is-wide' : 'loom-main')],
    [
      h.article(
        [h.Class('loom-prose'), h.Key(page.slug)],
        [
          h.div(
            [h.Class('loom-breadcrumbs')],
            [
              ...(page.part ? [h.span([], [page.part]), h.span([h.Class('loom-crumb-sep')], ['›'])] : []),
              h.span([h.Class('is-current')], [page.title]),
            ],
          ),
          ...Array.map(page.blocks, blockView),
        ],
      ),
    ],
  )

blocks.markdown: The docs shell keys the grid on the model's flags — a closed side becomes a `no-left` or `no-right` class. The view matches the route: a landing hands off to the landing module, a chapter builds the shell. The runtime mounts the app and drives the loop, handing it the address bar: a URL change arrives as `ChangedUrl`, a link click as `ClickedLink`.

blocks.markdown: The model is the shared ground the whole app stands on — the state, the messages, and the routing that maps the address bar to a view. Both halves of the site read it: the reading app that lays out the book, and the landing that fronts it. Keeping it in one module gives each a clean boundary. Neither reaches into the other; both reach into here.

blocks.title: The woven book and the styles

blocks.markdown: The app renders a `WovenCorpus` — the shape the weave pass produces for one corpus — so it imports that schema from `@athrio/loom-lang` and decodes the built data against it. Any drift between what the book produces and what the app expects fails here, at the decode, rather than rendering wrong. This module also pulls in the stylesheet, so importing the model brings the design system with it.

blocks.code:
import { Array, Option, pipe, Schema as S } from 'effect'
import { Navigation } from '@athrio/foldkit'
import { html } from '@athrio/foldkit/html'
import { m } from '@athrio/foldkit/message'
import { WovenCorpusSchema } from '@athrio/loom-lang/weave/WovenCorpus'
import siteData from './data/site.json'
import './styles.css'

export const site = S.decodeUnknownSync(WovenCorpusSchema)(siteData)

blocks.markdown: The site has two kinds of page, so it has two routes. `Landing` is the front page at `/`. `Docs` is a chapter, carrying the slug that names it. Modelling the route as a tagged union rather than a nullable slug means the view matches on it exhaustively — a landing can never be mistaken for a chapter with an empty name.

blocks.code:
export const RouteSchema = S.Union([
  S.Struct({ _tag: S.tag('Landing') }),
  S.Struct({ _tag: S.tag('Docs'), slug: S.String }),
])
export type Route = typeof RouteSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: The `Model` is the whole of the app's state: the route it shows, the theme it wears, and whether each side column is closed. The messages are what can happen to it. `ChangedUrl` sets the route from a new address; `ClickedLink` carries a link the runtime intercepted; `ToggledTheme` flips light and dark; `ToggledNav` and `ToggledToc` hide the sidebar and the outline; `Copy` asks for text to reach the clipboard; `CompletedNavigation` is the quiet reply a command returns.

The app is driven by the address bar, so it needs to move between a URL and a route. `pathForSlug` builds a chapter's clean URL, dropping the `NN-` ordering prefixes so the address reads plainly. `firstSlug` is the first chapter in the book, where the docs open. `slugForPath` maps a path back to its chapter, falling back to that first one; `routeForPath` wraps it — `/` is the landing, anything else is the chapter it names.
The model module is the woven book it decodes, the route, the state and its messages, and the mapping between addresses and routes.

blocks.markdown:
The `bun run release` command runs this script to release the `@athrio/loom-cli` package. The script tests first — a failure aborts before anything changes — then builds, bumps the minor version, re-tangles with the freshly built `loom` binary, and publishes.

The tangle step copies the version field from the CLI's manifest chapter into `package.json`, so editing `package.json` by hand would be overwritten on the next tangle. The version must therefore be bumped in the manifest chapter itself — this script does that.

The script lives in the `scripts/` directory and derives the package root and the manifest chapter's path from its own file path. The `run` helper invokes each command from the package root via `execFileSync`, inheriting stdio so output streams to the terminal. A non-zero exit code aborts the release.

blocks.code:
import { execFileSync } from 'node:child_process'
import { readFileSync, writeFileSync } from 'node:fs'
import { dirname, resolve } from 'node:path'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'

const cli = resolve(dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)), '..')
const manifest = resolve(
  cli,
  '../../corpus/08-loom-builds-loom/10-packaging-loom-cli.loom',
)

const run = (cmd: string, args: ReadonlyArray<string>): void =>
  execFileSync(cmd, [...args], { cwd: cli, stdio: 'inherit' })

blocks.markdown: The release runs the tests first. A failure aborts the script immediately, before any version is bumped or the package is published.

blocks.code: run('bun', ['test'])

blocks.markdown: The `bumpMinor` function increments the minor version in the manifest chapter, resets the patch number to zero, and throws when the version string is missing.

The script builds the CLI and re-tangles the manifest chapter with the freshly built `loom` binary — never the in-progress source — so the bumped version reaches `package.json`. It then publishes. The bump leaves the manifest chapter uncommitted, so the caller commits it after publishing.

blocks.code:
const version = after.match(/"version": "([^"]+)"/)?.[1] ?? '?'
run('bun', ['run', 'build'])
run('bun', ['dist/main.js', 'tangle', manifest])
run('bun', ['publish'])
console.log(`released @athrio/loom-cli ${version}`)

blocks.markdown: Vite bundles the language server entry, `src/index.ts`, into a single `dist/loom-server.js`. The bundle targets Node 20 in CommonJS form, the shape a Node host loads it as.

Some of the server's dependencies — the `vscode-*-languageservice` packages and `jsonc-parser` — ship only UMD bundles, the older module format that rolldown cannot follow statically. The `umd2esm` plugin rewrites each such import to its ESM sibling so the bundler can inline it. This is the same trick volarjs/starter uses.

`vscode-markdown-languageservice`, which `ProseLanguage`'s Markdown service pulls in, imports `vscode-uri` by its default binding, which that package's ES-module build does not provide. An alias resolves `vscode-uri` to its UMD build instead, where the bundler's CommonJS interop supplies one.
Node's built-in modules stay external — they are present in every Node runtime — and everything else is inlined. The single entry builds to `dist/loom-server.js`.

`@athrio/loom-service-typescript` is the first loadable language service. A project installs it to give its TypeScript product an editor program of its own, held apart from the frame's. The package builds to one ES module, `dist/index.js`, whose default export is the service — the artifact the dynamic loader imports at runtime.

The `exports` field names the package's surface, and it is a single entry: the default `.`, the built `dist/index.js` whose default export is the service the loader reads. The `files` field ships only `dist`, so the published package is that one built file and its source map, and `publishConfig` makes the scoped package public.

The package carries no dependencies. The build bundles `volar-service-typescript` and Volar's language layers into `dist/index.js`, and the runtime bridge reads `effect`, the `@athrio/loom-lang-services` contract, and the editor's TypeScript from the host at load. Those three are neither bundled nor installed — the host lends them, so a second copy never shadows the `TypescriptSdk` the host provides. The `devDependencies` exist only to build and type-check the source; what the running service shares with the host is fixed by the runtime version `Runtime` declares, not by an npm range.
The config extends the workspace base, which supplies the compiler options and the Node and Bun types, and typechecks `src`. It never ships.

The `@athrio/loom-cli` package ships to npm as a single self-contained file. Vite bundles the CLI program, the language server, and Effect into `dist/main.js` and adds a Bun shebang — a line that makes the file directly executable by Bun. The published package is therefore pure JavaScript with no runtime dependencies — every library it needs is already inside the bundle.

The `files` field restricts the published package to the `dist` directory, and the `bin` field maps the `loom` command to the bundle. The bundle inlines every dependency, so all dependencies belong in the `devDependencies` field.

The `build` script bundles the tangled source files committed to the repository. Tangling the corpus is a separate step, performed with the published `loom` CLI rather than the in-progress source — using an unverified tangler to tangle the corpus that produces it would be circular. The `prepack` script re-bundles automatically before each publish. The `bun publish` command rewrites the `workspace:*` specifier for `@athrio/loom-lang` — Bun's protocol for local package references — into the real published version number.

The `tsconfig.json` extends the workspace-root `tsconfig.base.json` and covers the `src` directory. Vite produces the `dist` bundle, so the configuration file is a development tool only and never ships with the published package.

Vite bundles the CLI program, the language server's TypeScript source, and Effect into a single `dist/main.js` module, adding a Bun shebang — a line that makes the file directly executable by Bun. The installed `loom` binary therefore carries no runtime dependencies and no TypeScript source to compile.

The `node:` built-in modules stay external: Bun supplies them at runtime, so the bundle need not carry them. The `bun` module stays external for the same reason — the platform adapter reaches the Bun runtime through it. Everything else, the language server's TypeScript source and Effect, is inlined into `dist/main.js`.

The language server's `exports` field points to TypeScript source files rather than pre-compiled output. Bun runs these files directly, as do tools like `tsx` and code editors, with no compilation step. Vite inlines them at build time, so the published CLI is pure JavaScript. The `loom lsp` command pulls in the server, and with it Volar; some of Volar's dependencies — the `vscode-*-languageservice` packages and `jsonc-parser` — ship only UMD bundles the bundler cannot follow statically, so the `umd2esm` plugin rewrites each to its ESM sibling, the same trick the language-server build uses.

`@athrio/loom-config` reads the configuration with the `yaml` parser, whose CommonJS build calls `require('process')`. An ES-module bundle exposes no `require`, so that call would throw at load time. The config resolves `yaml` to its ES-module build instead, which uses imports throughout and needs no `require`.

`ProseLanguage` loads `volar-service-markdown`, whose `vscode-markdown-languageservice` dependency imports `vscode-uri` by its default binding. The `vscode-uri` ES-module build exports no default, so the bundle resolves `vscode-uri` to its UMD build instead, where the bundler's CommonJS interop supplies one.

Vite's library mode builds a single entry point to `dist/main.js`. Bun runs the latest JavaScript, so the build targets `esnext` and down-levels nothing. The CLI package uses the ESM format — ECMAScript modules — so Vite emits an ESM bundle with a Bun shebang, making it directly executable. The VS Code extension runs in the editor's CommonJS host, so its Vite config emits that format instead.

The loader imports a built JavaScript file, not TypeScript source, so the package builds to one: `dist/index.js`, an ES module whose default export is the `TypescriptService`. The build folds the service's own modules and its Volar machinery into that single file, so the store holds a self-contained service with nothing left to resolve.

The one thing the service must not bundle is the runtime it shares with the host. A second copy of Effect would give the service its own `TypescriptSdk` tag, distinct from the one the host provides, and the dependency injection would never meet. So three specifiers stay out of the bundle — `effect`, the `LanguageService` contract, and `typescript`. The host lends those, and a small plugin rewrites their imports to read the host's live copies through the global the host writes. `Runtime` in `@athrio/loom-lang-services` names that boundary.

`loomRuntimeBridge` turns each shared specifier into a module that reads the host's runtime off the global. It resolves the specifier to a virtual id, then builds that module: it imports the real package once, at build time, to learn the names the package exports, and emits one binding per name, each reading the live value from `globalThis` under the runtime key. A service loaded with no host runtime in place fails with a clear message rather than a missing-property error.

The bridge runs only at build. It shapes the published bundle alone and leaves the test and dev pipelines, which read the same Vite config, to resolve the runtime the ordinary way.

The runtime's built-in modules stay external — every runtime carries them. Everything else is bundled, except the three specifiers the bridge intercepts — so `volar-service-typescript` and the rest of Volar are inlined, their CommonJS internals transformed in place, rather than left to resolve from a store that does not carry them. The one entry builds to `dist/index.js`, the ES module the loader's dynamic `import` reads.

Vite's `defineConfig` shapes the build and its `Plugin` type types the bridge. The `builtinModules` list names the specifiers to leave external, `dirname` and `resolve` locate the entry, and `fileURLToPath` reads this config's own directory.

The bridge needs two values from the runtime contract: `runtimeKey`, the global property the host writes, and `runtimeSpecifiers`, the specifiers it covers. `Runtime` in `@athrio/loom-lang-services` is their home, but a build tool cannot import that TypeScript source, so the config declares them again and the conformance test holds the two copies in step.

import { defineConfig, type Plugin } from 'vite'
import { builtinModules } from 'node:module'
import { dirname, resolve } from 'node:path'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'

const here = dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url))
const nodeBuiltins = [
  ...builtinModules,
  ...builtinModules.map((name) => `node:${name}`),
]

const runtimeKey = '__loomRuntime'
const runtimeSpecifiers = [
  'effect',
  '@athrio/loom-lang-services/LanguageService',
  'typescript',
]

blocks.markdown: The `@athrio/loom-cli` package is the command-line tool for Loom: it tangles `.loom` files into source, scaffolds a workspace's configuration, and activates the language services it uses. The tool is itself written in Loom.

`loom init` opens with the Loom wordmark, so the tool announces itself before it asks anything. `banner` prints the wordmark in teal above a dim tagline. `teal` and `dim` wrap a string in the ANSI escape codes a terminal renders as colour, then reset, so whatever prints next is plain again.

The `tangle` command takes a file path, resolves it against the working directory, and hands it to the tangler. The tangler walks the file and emits each output; the command logs every path it writes. When the tangler refuses a file — an unresolved `::[…]` anchor would otherwise drop silently from the output — the command prints the error and exits non-zero, writing nothing.

The `weave` command projects a corpus into data. It takes the corpus entry and an output path, resolves both against the working directory, hands them to the weaver, and reports the file it wrote. The weaver asks the compiler to project the whole corpus into a `WovenCorpus` and writes it as JSON — one file a consumer renders however it likes, static or live.

`loom init` sets up a workspace. It writes `.loom/config.yaml` — the compiled configuration the editor and the tangler read — and a `.gitignore` beside it that keeps the generated service store out of version control. It asks for the primary language a specifier-less section inherits, and for the languages the editor activates services for, then records them. When a `.loom/config.yaml` is already there, it asks before overwriting, so a stray `init` never erases a configured workspace.

The primary language defaults to TypeScript, and the activation answer defaults to that same language, so a TypeScript workspace is two key-presses away. The activation answer is a space-separated list, since one workspace may run several languages. A prompt the author quits — with `Ctrl-C` — fails with `QuitError`, which `init` catches to leave with a word rather than a stack trace.

`loom add <language>` turns a language on: it installs that language's service into the store and records the language among the package's activated set. `loom remove <language>` turns it off, taking the service out of the store and the id out of the config.

The install runs only when the store lacks the service, so re-adding an installed language just records it, with no package manager and no network. An install that fails leaves with the reason and a non-zero exit, the config untouched, because a language whose service never arrived must not read as activated.

Both commands record the change the same way. `updateLanguages` reads the workspace's current manifest, rewrites its activated languages, and writes it back. It keeps the corpus directory, the primary, the anchor, the per-language settings, and any package overrides the manifest already holds, so turning a language on or off changes only the set of languages. An activated language keeps its existing entry — a service override among it — while a new one starts empty.

`loom status` shows the state of activation at a glance: where the store sits, the primary language a specifier-less section inherits, and the activated languages. Beside each activated language it marks whether the service is installed — a filled dot when the store holds it, a hollow one and the `loom add` that would fix it when the store does not.

The CLI is seven commands. `loom tangle <path>` emits a `.loom`'s files, the path a file or a directory of them. `loom weave <path> <out>` projects a corpus into a `WovenCorpus` and writes it as JSON to `out`. `loom init [dir]` scaffolds a `.loom/config.yaml`, defaulting to the working directory. `loom add <language>` and `loom remove <language>` turn a language's service on and off. `loom status` reports what the workspace has activated and what the store holds. `loom lsp --stdio` starts the language server for any editor. `@effect/cli` parses the line, dispatches to the matching command, and prints help when none matches. The run site provides every service the commands reach for — the tangler and its disk source, the package config, the configuration, and the Bun platform behind the prompts and the filesystem.

The `lsp` branch sits outside the command program. It loads the server lazily, so a plain `tangle` never pays to bundle Volar, and the server runs on its own Bun runtime rather than nesting one inside a command. Volar is CommonJS and calls `require` at runtime for the built-in modules; this bundle is an ES module, where `require` is absent, so before loading the server the branch exposes one through `createRequire` on `globalThis` — the shim Volar's bundled code reaches for. The cost lands only on the `lsp` path.

The program is built on Effect, a functional TypeScript library for composing and running effectful programs. Effect's CLI toolkit parses the command line and drives the interactive prompts, and the Bun platform supplies the runtime, the terminal, and filesystem access. The language server contributes four services: `LoomTangler`, which walks a `.loom` file and emits the tangled output; `LoomWeaver`, which projects a corpus and writes the woven data; `DocumentSource`, which feeds them source text from disk; and `PackageConfig`, which resolves a `.loom`'s package settings. From `@athrio/loom-config` comes `LoomConfig`, which resolves a file's configuration, reads the whole compiled manifest, and writes it back. From `@athrio/loom-lang-services` come the store operations behind activation: `addService` and `removeService` install and uninstall a language's package, `installedServices` lists what the store holds, `storeDir` locates it, `workspaceRoot` finds the workspace it sits in, and `servicePackage` names a language's package.

import { Argument, Command, Prompt } from 'effect/unstable/cli'

import { existsSync, writeFileSync } from 'node:fs'
import { resolve as resolvePath } from 'node:path'

} from '@athrio/loom-lang-services/LoomStore'

blocks.markdown: The `@athrio/loom-lang` package is the language behind every `.loom` file — the Volar extended language and the engine that tangles it. The command-line tool and the VS Code extension both build on it: they import its source and bundle it into their own output, so this package exposes TypeScript source rather than a precompiled library.

Two field groups carry the package's shape. The `imports` field defines one package subpath alias, `#ast/*`, for the AST source, so internal imports stay stable rather than relative. The `exports` field names the public entry points — `./LoomCompiler`, `./LoomTangler`, `./PackageConfig`, `./ManifestBuilder`, and `./LoomServer` — each pointing at a source file the consumer inlines. `./weave/WovenCorpus` exposes the woven-corpus schema on its own, so the reader site types its rendered pages against the same definition the weave pass produces rather than a hand-kept copy, and `./weave/LoomWeaver` exposes the writer the CLI drives to emit that data. `@athrio/loom-ast` supplies the shared domain types the verbs build and the toolchain reads, a workspace dependency.

The `main` and `bin` fields point at built output: `dist/loom-server.js` is the bundled language server, and the `loom-language-server` binary launches it. The runtime libraries the server needs — Volar, Effect, the `volar-service-*` plugins — are `dependencies`. `@athrio/loom-service-typescript` is a `devDependency`: the server loads it at runtime from the Loom store rather than bundling it, so it is present only to build and test against. `vscode-uri` is a type-only `devDependency`.
The `tsconfig.json` extends the workspace-root `tsconfig.base.json` and covers both the `src` and `test` directories. Vite produces the `dist` bundle, so the configuration is a development tool only.

`@athrio/loom-ast` is Loom's domain model — the pure data every other package shares. It holds every AST a `.loom` file passes through: the `LoomNode` foundation of positions and health, the parse `LoomDocument`, the `LoomCorpusAst`, the de re the product pass builds (`Code`, `File`, `Fragment`), the `LoomSymbol` registry that names each token's editor capabilities, the `LoomVirtualCode` the editor reads, and the grammar's `Weft` and `LoomTokens`. It is a leaf: it carries data and the pure readers over it, never a builder or a runner, and depends on nothing of Loom — only on Effect's `Schema`. So `@athrio/loom-lang` and each language service rest on it without a cycle. Like the rest of Loom, it ships TypeScript source with no build step.

The `exports` map names one entry point per model. There is no default entry: the builders and runners that construct these nodes live in `@athrio/loom-lang`, not here. The `imports` map defines one internal alias, `#ast/*`, so a model reaches its siblings by a stable name. `effect` is a peer dependency, shared with whoever installs the package rather than carried twice.

The `tsconfig.json` extends the workspace-root `tsconfig.base.json` and covers `src` and `test`. The package ships source with no build step, so the configuration type-checks the models and their tests and emits nothing.
`@athrio/loom-config` ships as source — no build of its own.

The `exports` field is the package's surface — one entry, the `LoomConfig` module. The CLI
and the language server inline this source when they bundle; `effect` is a peer,
supplied by the host bundle. The `yaml` parser, which reads the compiled
configuration, is a real dependency, bundled along with the source.

The config extends the workspace base and typechecks `src`. The Node and Bun types come from the
base, which declares `types: ['node', 'bun']` once for every package — Bun's isolated
layout does not reliably resolve `@types/node`. The config never ships.
`@athrio/loom-lang-services` ships as source — no build of its own.

The `exports` field is the package's surface: the `LanguageService` contract, the
`LanguageRegistry`, the `LanguageLoader` that resolves a project's active services
from the Loom store, the `LoomStore` that installs and holds them, the
`Runtime` the host lends a loaded service, and the two built-in services,
`LoomLanguage` for the Frame and `ProseLanguage` for the prose.

`LoomLanguage` wraps `volar-service-typescript` and `ProseLanguage` wraps
`volar-service-markdown` — the two real dependencies. The other
peers — `@athrio/loom-ast` for Loom's shared types, `effect`,
`@volar/language-service`, `typescript`, and `vscode-uri` for decoding an embedded
document's uri — are supplied by the host bundle.

blocks.markdown:
The TypeScript config extends the workspace base — strict mode, the Node and Bun types — and checks
`src`. Like the package, it never ships.

The `loom-vscode` package ships to the VS Code Marketplace as a `.vsix` bundle. Vite bundles the client and the language server into `dist/client.js` and `dist/server.js`, and `vsce` — the Marketplace packaging tool — wraps the result into the `.vsix`. Every library the extension needs is inlined into the bundle, so the published package carries no runtime dependencies.

VS Code reads the manifest to learn what the extension is and when to run it. The `activationEvents` field loads the extension the first time a `loom` file opens, and the `contributes.languages` field registers the `.loom` extension as the `loom` language so that event can fire. The `main` field points the extension host at the bundled client.

The `pack:vsix` script builds the bundle and then packages it with `vsce`. The bundle inlines every dependency, so all dependencies belong in the `devDependencies` field — nothing is installed at the user's end.

The `tsconfig.json` extends the workspace-root `tsconfig.base.json` and covers the `src` directory. It adds the `node` and `vscode` type packages, since the extension runs in the VS Code host. Vite produces the `dist` bundle, so the configuration is a development tool only and never ships in the `.vsix`.

Vite bundles the extension for the VS Code extension host. It has two entry points — the client in this package and the language server in `@athrio/loom-lang` — and emits them as `dist/client.js` and `dist/server.js`. The extension host loads CommonJS, so the build emits CommonJS rather than the ESM the CLI produces.

Some of the server's dependencies — the `vscode-*-languageservice` packages and `jsonc-parser` — ship only UMD bundles, the older module format that rolldown cannot follow statically. The `umd2esm` plugin rewrites each such import to its ESM sibling so the bundler can inline it. This is the same trick volarjs/starter uses.

`vscode-markdown-languageservice`, which `ProseLanguage`'s Markdown service pulls in, imports `vscode-uri` by its default binding, which that package's ES-module build does not provide. An alias resolves `vscode-uri` to its UMD build instead, where the bundler's CommonJS interop supplies one.

The `vscode` module is provided by the host at runtime, and Node's built-in modules are present in every Node runtime, so both stay external — everything else is inlined. The two entries build to `dist/client.js` and `dist/server.js`, targeting Node 20 in CommonJS form.

Loom runs on Bun — the CLI, the tangler, the tests. An editor is where it meets a runtime it does not choose: an IDE loads an extension into its own *host*, whose runtime is the editor's. VS Code's is Node, so this extension is the one place Node remains — everywhere else, Loom is a Bun program.

The `loom-vscode` extension is Loom's client for that host. When VS Code opens a `.loom` file, the extension starts the Loom language server, `@athrio/loom-lang`, in a separate Node process and connects to it, so the server's diagnostics, hovers, and completions appear in the editor. The build bundles the server beside this client into the extension's `dist` directory. The same server, started by `loom lsp`, runs on Bun instead.

blocks.title: What the client is built on

blocks.markdown: The extension draws on three libraries. The `vscode` module is the editor's own extension API. The `vscode-languageclient` library runs the client half of the Language Server Protocol — the contract an editor and a language server talk over — and reaches the server through a Node IPC channel. Volar's VS Code helpers add auto-insertion and register the client with the Volar Labs inspector.

blocks.markdown: Both `activate` and `deactivate` need the same language client — one to start it, the other to stop it — so it lives in a single module-level binding.

blocks.markdown:
VS Code calls `activate` the first time a `.loom` file opens. The function points the client at the bundled `server.js` and launches it over IPC, under two profiles: a plain `run` profile, and a `debug` profile that opens an inspector port for attaching a debugger.

The server type-checks the synthesised frame, so it needs a TypeScript `lib/` directory. Rather than bundle one, the client points the server at the TypeScript that VS Code already ships — `loadTsdkByPath` on the server loads `typescript.js` from there and resolves the `lib.*.d.ts` files beside it.

The client is scoped to the `loom` language. Once it has started, the function wires auto-insertion for `loom` documents and registers the client with the Volar Labs inspector, then returns the inspector's exports.
When VS Code unloads the extension, `deactivate` stops the client, which shuts the server process down. The client may never have started, so the call is guarded.

links.name: What the client is built on

blocks.markdown:
A package turns a language on by naming its id in `loom.json` — `typescript`, `python`, `go`. Loom itself is the one language built into the server. Every other language ships as a `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` package the project installs. This module turns each activated id into the `LanguageService` that serves it: it finds the package the project installed, imports it, and confirms the package exports a service for that id.

A language that fails to load costs only itself. Its sections stay at syntax highlighting — the floor every section keeps, with or without a service — while every other language serves on. Activating a language whose package is missing degrades the editor; it never breaks it.

The loader resolves a package from the store `storeDir` locates, imports it, and reads the package's default export. The import is dynamic — by name, at runtime — because a package is a unit the consumer installs after the editor ships, never one a build could fold in.

What the import returns is unknown until checked. `isLanguageService` confirms the default export has the shape the contract requires, and the loader confirms the service's declared `id` is the one activated. A package that answers to another id, or exports something that is not a service at all, is not the language the project asked for. When the package is missing or fails either check, the loader logs the reason and yields nothing.

`loadActive` produces the services a project has turned on. Loom and Prose lead the set — every `.loom` has a frame and prose, so both services are always present — and each activated id follows, loaded by `loadService`. The loader drops an id equal to a built-in's, so naming `loom` or `prose` in the config never doubles it, and drops a repeated id, so activating a language twice loads it once. An id whose package does not load contributes nothing, and the set carries the languages that did.

blocks.code:
export const loadActive = (
  ids: ReadonlyArray<string>,
  root: string,
): Effect.Effect<ReadonlyArray<LanguageService>> =>
  pipe(
    ids,
    Array.filter((id) => id !== LoomLanguage.id && id !== ProseLanguage.id),
    Array.dedupe,
    (rest) => Effect.forEach(rest, (id) => loadService(id, root)),
    Effect.map((loaded) => [
      LoomLanguage,
      ProseLanguage,
      ...Array.getSomes(loaded),
    ]),
  )

blocks.markdown: The loader works in Effect: `Effect` runs the load, `Option` carries a language that may not load, and `Array` with `pipe` build the set. Bun loads the package — `createRequire` resolves it within the store, `pathToFileURL` forms the specifier the dynamic import takes, and `dirname` with `join` point the resolver at the store's directory. From `./LoomStore` come `servicePackage`, which names a language's package, and `storeDir`, which locates the project's store. From the contract module come the `LanguageService` type and the `isLanguageService` guard; `LoomLanguage` and `ProseLanguage` are the built-in services that always lead the set.

blocks.markdown:
`TypescriptService` is the loadable language service for product TypeScript. Where `LoomLanguage` checks the frame in Volar's built-in program, this service checks each TypeScript section against a `ProductProgram` of its own — one the consumer's `tsconfig.json` governs, not the frame's baseline. A package activates it by naming `typescript` in its `loom.json`.

For each request the editor sends, the service finds the Product the document belongs to — a composition root with its members folded in — hands that Product's resolved TypeScript to the program, and forwards the request there. The program answers in the Product's own coordinates, and Volar maps the answer back to the `.loom`.

The product program checks against the consumer's own compiler options, so the service finds them the way `tsc` does: it walks up from the `.loom` file for the nearest `tsconfig.json` and parses it. A package with no `tsconfig.json` falls back to a baseline — modern modules, `strict` on — so a section still checks.

Either way the service forces two options. It forces `noEmit`, because the editor analyzes the product and never writes it. And it allows `.ts` import extensions, because Loom runs the product directly under Bun, with no build between the source and the run, so a cross-file `import` names the `.ts` file it resolves to rather than a `.js` that is never emitted.

blocks.title: Choosing a program for a section

blocks.markdown:
A request arrives on an embedded document — one Product of a `.loom`, a composition root with its members already folded in. `productTarget` decodes that document's URI back to the `.loom` path and the Product's id, and serves only a TypeScript-family document — `typescript`, `tsx`, `javascript`, or `jsx`. It declines the rest, so a JSON or CSS Product the `.loom` also holds falls to its own service. It then reads the file's composition — every root in the corpus that tangles to a file, at the path it tangles to — and looks for the one this document edits.

A root that tangles to a file is checked at that path, the live buffer overriding what is on disk. A root that tangles nowhere — a section with no file sink — has no path of its own, so `productTarget` checks it beside its `.loom` under a synthetic name. Either way it hands the program the whole composition, so one root's `import` of another resolves against the other's composition. It returns the edited root's path for the caller to query.

`productPlugin` is the Volar plugin the host registers. It keeps one `ProductProgram` per `.loom` file — built on first use from that file's consumer options — and disposes them all when the service shuts down. It reads the composed corpus from the `Composition` capability the host provides. Each feature routes its document through `productTarget`: when the document is a TypeScript Product, the feature forwards to its program; otherwise it returns nothing, and a document in another language falls to its own service or to syntax highlighting. The TypeScript analysis itself is `volar-service-typescript`'s, run inside the program.

A result that names a place — a definition, a reference, the edits of a rename or a code action — comes back under the path the program checks. `embeddedUriOf` rewrites that path to the embedded document Volar knows: it finds the root the program holds there and encodes its `.loom` and id back through `encodeEmbeddedDocumentUri`. Volar then maps the result to the `.loom`, rather than opening a file that may not be on disk. A path that is no root the program holds — a real file under `node_modules` — passes through unchanged.

Go-to on an `import` specifier is the one result that names a whole file rather than a place in it: TypeScript points it at the module's start, `(0, 0)`. `remapDefinition` catches that. When a definition lands at the start of a composition root, it redirects to that root's `heading` — the section that tangles the file — so the author arrives at `# The temperature library`, not the transcluded line the composed file opens with. Every other definition takes the `embeddedUriOf` path.

A code action carries edits too, but a refactor leaves its edit unfilled until it is resolved. So `provideCodeActions` resolves any action that arrives without one, then moves every edit's file paths back to the `.loom` through `embeddedUriOf`. An `Add import` quick-fix already carries its edit — the `import` of the missing name, computed in the product's own coordinates — so only its paths cross back. Resolving inside the program keeps each edit on the product's side of the boundary.

`TypescriptService` is the `LanguageService` the host activates for `typescript`. Its `plugins` Effect takes the editor's TypeScript compiler from `TypescriptSdk` and the composed corpus from `Composition`, then builds the one plugin. Where the frame rides Volar's shared TypeScript program, this service owns a program of its own.

The package exports the service as its default. That default is the entry the dynamic loader reads: `LanguageLoader` imports the built package and takes `default`, expecting a value that satisfies the `LanguageService` contract under the id `typescript`.

The service builds on `@athrio/loom-lang-services` for the `LanguageService` contract, the `TypescriptSdk` and `Composition` capabilities, and the `ComposedFile` shape each composition root takes; and on its own `ProductProgram`. From Effect it takes `Effect` for the `plugins` body, and `Array`, `Option`, and `pipe` to search the composition; from Volar, the `LanguageServicePlugin` type, with `CodeAction`, `WorkspaceEdit`, `LocationLink`, and `Range` for the results and edits the plugin moves back to the `.loom`; the `URI` type to decode an embedded document back to its `.loom`; and Bun's `dirname` to walk for a `tsconfig.json`.

links.name: Choosing a program for a section

blocks.markdown:
The frame keeps Volar's built-in TypeScript program. The product needs one of its own. `createProductProgram` builds it: a standalone TypeScript service, governed by the consumer's `tsconfig.json` rather than the frame's baked baseline, so each section is analyzed the way the project ships it.

The program never opens a `.loom`. The host composes the file's whole corpus — every composition root, resolved to TypeScript — and hands the program each root under its own path. One root's `import` of another then resolves against that other's composition, not its last output on disk. The same `volar-service-typescript` plugins the frame uses answer diagnostics, hover, completion, and definition over this program. The host maps each answer back to its place in the `.loom`.

A `ProductProgram` holds the product's roots and answers the editor's questions about them. `sync` sets those roots in one call. It takes the file's composition — each root under its own path — then adds the roots new since the last call, replaces the ones whose text changed, and drops the ones now gone. The feature methods answer over a root by its path: `diagnostics` for the whole file; `hover`, `completions`, `definition`, and `references` at a position; `rename` for a new name; and `codeActions` for the quick-fixes and refactors in a range, with `resolveCodeAction` filling in an action's edit. Each returns what `volar-service-typescript` finds, named by that path. The service rewrites each such path to the embedded document it stands for, and Volar maps the coordinates back to the `.loom`. `dispose` releases the service.

blocks.code:
export interface ProductProgram {
  readonly sync: (
    files: ReadonlyArray<{ readonly path: string; readonly text: string }>,
  ) => void
  readonly diagnostics: (fileName: string) => Promise<Diagnostic[]>
  readonly hover: (
    fileName: string,
    position: Position,
  ) => Promise<Hover | undefined>
  readonly completions: (
    fileName: string,
    position: Position,
  ) => Promise<CompletionList>
  readonly definition: (
    fileName: string,
    position: Position,
  ) => Promise<LocationLink[] | undefined>
  readonly references: (
    fileName: string,
    position: Position,
  ) => Promise<Location[] | undefined>
  readonly rename: (
    fileName: string,
    position: Position,
    newName: string,
  ) => Promise<WorkspaceEdit | undefined>
  readonly codeActions: (
    fileName: string,
    range: Range,
    context: CodeActionContext,
  ) => Promise<CodeAction[] | undefined>
  readonly resolveCodeAction: (action: CodeAction) => Promise<CodeAction>
  readonly dispose: () => void
}

blocks.markdown: Volar reads files through a language, a registry of snapshots it keeps in sync. `productLanguage` builds one that serves the product roots from memory and every other file from disk. It answers a root by file name, from the `roots` map the program owns. Every other file Volar asks for — the TypeScript standard library, a package under `node_modules` — it reads through `ts.sys`, the TypeScript system interface. A file that is neither a root nor present on disk leaves the registry.

`createProductProgram` ties the layers together. It owns the `roots` map and a project version, both of which `sync` keeps current: `sync` drops the roots the new set omits, writes the ones whose text changed, and bumps the version once when either happened. The program then rechecks after a real edit and rests otherwise. `unchanged` is the guard `sync` uses, comparing a root's incoming text against the snapshot already held. The project host reports the compiler options, that version, and the root file names. `createLanguageServiceHost` turns it into the host TypeScript needs. `createLanguageService` runs the feature plugins over that host. The returned `ProductProgram` forwards each feature to it, naming the file with `URI.file` and passing the position through unchanged.

blocks.markdown: The product program is a Volar language service assembled by hand. `createLanguage` registers the files, `createLanguageServiceHost` turns them into a TypeScript program, and `createLanguageService` runs the feature plugins over it. `resolveFileLanguageId` reads a file's language from its extension, so a `.ts` file held in memory counts as TypeScript. The `URI` type names every file, and `volar-service-typescript` brings the editor features. From Effect come `Array`, `Option`, and `pipe`, which `sync` uses to reconcile the roots.

blocks.markdown:
`@athrio/loom-lang-services` holds the contract every Loom language service satisfies — the `LanguageService` — together with the loader that finds them and the one service built into the server. A `LanguageService` gives one language its editor support: its diagnostics, hover, and completion.

Loom is the one language built in. `LoomLanguage` serves the frame, and every `.loom` has a frame, so its service is always present and needs no package. Every other language — TypeScript first among them — ships as a `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` package the project installs and the loader resolves by id. This module defines the contract both kinds satisfy; `LanguageLoader` turns an activated id into the service that serves it.

Each service gives one embedded language its editor support — diagnostics, hover, completion — and every service, built in or loaded, is the same `LanguageService`. Loom is the one **built-in**: `LoomLanguage` lives here as a module, with no package. Every other language is **loaded**: each ships in its own `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` package, installed on activation and imported by id. TypeScript is the first such package, not a privileged built-in; it goes through the loader like any other language.

The `@athrio/loom-lang` language server reads the active ids from `loom.config.json`, and `LanguageLoader` resolves each one to its service — Loom from this module, the rest from their packages. The server indexes the resolved set in the `LanguageRegistry`, keyed by `id` for a section's declared language and by extension for a tangle path, so finding a language is a lookup, never a branch.

Editor features take a different route. The host runs each service's `plugins` Effect
to collect its Volar plugins and registers them; Volar then dispatches every request
to the right language by its `languageId`. Loom declares the languages; Volar routes
them.

```
  loom activate ──►  loom.config.json   { languages: [typescript, css, go] }
                            │ read on initialize
                            ▼
  @athrio/loom-lang — the language server

    LanguageLoader resolves each active id to a LanguageService:

      built-in, in the server:   loom — the frame
      loaded, one package each:  everything else, by id —
        typescript → @athrio/loom-service-typescript → tsc
        go         → @athrio/loom-service-go         → gopls
        rust       → @athrio/loom-service-rust       → rust-analyzer
        (third-party packages too)

    for each active id: take loom, or import @athrio/loom-service-<id>;
    run service.plugins(ctx) and register its plugins under the id — a
    lookup, never a per-language branch.

                            │ both kinds satisfy one shape
                            ▼

  @athrio/loom-lang-services
    LanguageService { id, displayName, extensions, plugins(ctx) → Effect<Plugin[]> }
    isLanguageService · defineLanguageService
    LanguageLoader.loadActive(ids, root)

  Routing: a section tagged id="css" → the css service → hover / diagnostics /
  completion → mapped back to its span in the .loom.
```

The host hands a service its `settings` and runs `plugins`, providing the
capabilities the Effect requires — today, the TypeScript SDK. An in-process service
builds its plugins in memory; an external one spawns its binary and bridges it —
both inside the Effect, so the collect-and-register pass is the same for every
language.

The contract names Volar's `LanguageServicePlugin`, the unit of editor behavior; from
Effect, `Data` for the tagged error and `Effect` itself, since a service builds its
plugins in an Effect and a capability is an Effect service; and from
`@athrio/loom-ast`, the `Diagnostic` the frame query returns and the `SemanticToken`
colour it tags each of a file's tokens with.

blocks.code:
import type { LanguageServicePlugin } from '@volar/language-service'
import { Context, Data, Effect, Layer } from 'effect'
import { type Diagnostic } from '@athrio/loom-ast/LoomNode'
import type { SemanticToken } from '@athrio/loom-ast/LoomSymbol'

blocks.markdown:
A `LanguageService` is one language's entry: the section `id` it serves, a
`displayName` for the `loom activate` menu, its file `extensions`, and `plugins` —
the Effect that builds its Volar plugins. That Effect may fail with a `ServiceError`
— an external binary that will not start — which the host reports without
bringing the other languages down. A service may draw on the host's capabilities
(`HostCapabilities`): the Loom and TypeScript services take the TypeScript SDK, a
CSS service takes nothing. A tangle path's extension names its language — a section that tangles to
`main.ts` turns on the TypeScript service.

A service may draw on capabilities the host provides, taken through the Effect environment so each service asks only for what it needs. There are three.

`TypescriptSdk` is the TypeScript compiler itself. It carries no default — the host supplies the editor's own instance (via Volar's `loadTsdkByPath`), so analysis matches the user's project. The Loom and TypeScript services both take it; `create` needs the compiler to check the frame and the TypeScript product. A CSS or Go service never asks.

`FrameQuery` is the host's read into the frame — the questions Loom can answer about a file from the composition it built. Its first reader, `diagnostics`, returns that file's Loom diagnostics, the grammatical and semantic health in Loom's own terms, so the frame service can surface them. Four more readers answer navigation and rename. `definition` gives the heading an anchor at an offset resolves to, and `references` gives every anchor that names the section under an offset. `rename` gives the title and anchor-name spans a rename rewrites together, and `renameRange` gives the one span the rename box covers. These answer in `FrameLocation`s — a path and a range in the editor's own line-and-character coordinates. A last reader, `semanticTokens`, gives each of a file's tokens its colour: a `FrameToken` pairing a range with the `SemanticToken` kind `LoomSymbol` assigns, so the editor highlights the source. The host builds the query over the warm corpus, and like `TypescriptSdk` it carries no default. Only the Loom service takes it.

`Composition` is the host's read of the de re as a filesystem: every composition root in a file's corpus, each as the resolved code it tangles to, named by the path it tangles to. A product service builds its analyzer's program over these, so a cross-file `import` resolves to another section's live composition rather than its last-tangled output on disk. Each root also carries its `heading`, the `FrameLocation` of the section that tangles it, so go-to on that `import` lands on the section in the `.loom` rather than the transcluded code the composed file opens with. Like the others it carries no default; the host builds it over the warm corpus. The product service takes it.

A product service still asks nothing about its own file's sections: the editor hands it each Product directly — a resolved root, its members folded in — so the file's own roots travel with the data, never a query. `Composition` adds only the rest of the corpus, the roots a file imports across.

`HostCapabilities` is the union of these capabilities — the environment a service's `plugins` Effect may require.

The `LanguageServiceContext` is the one input the host hands every service: `settings`, this
language's own entry in `loom.config.json`. The service decodes it — format options
for an in-process service, the binary command for an external one. Capabilities like
the TypeScript SDK do not come through the context; they come through the environment, so the
context keeps the same shape for every language.

Each service declares itself with `defineLanguageService`, which checks the value against the contract and returns it unchanged. A built-in module and a package's default export pass through the same check, so the two are the same kind of value however they reach the server. `isLanguageService` is that check, and the loader reuses it on a package it imports — there the value is unknown until proven. The guard holds when the value carries a string `id` and `displayName`, an array of `extensions`, and a `plugins` function.

blocks.markdown:
A loaded language service ships only its own code. It carries no Effect, no service contract, and no TypeScript compiler of its own — it borrows the host's, so the capability the host provides and the one the service asks for are the same instance rather than two copies that never meet. This module is the boundary where the host lends that runtime.

The host writes its live modules to a global the moment it starts. A service built to read that global, instead of importing the runtime by name, then runs on the host's exact instances. This is the literal form of the rule that the host owns the runtime, made mechanical: one Effect, one contract, one compiler, shared across the load.

Three names fix the boundary. `runtimeKey` is the global property the host writes and a service reads. `runtimeVersion` marks the shape of what is shared, so a service built against a newer host can tell. `runtimeSpecifiers` is the exact set of module specifiers the bridge covers — the imports a service resolves from the host rather than from its own files.

The set is deliberately small. It holds only what must be one shared instance: `effect`, because the host runs the service's `plugins` in its own runtime; the `LanguageService` contract, because the `TypescriptSdk` capability and the service brand are keyed by it; and `typescript`, so a service analyzes the product with the editor's compiler. Everything else a service needs — Volar's machinery, the `URI` type — it bundles, because those cross the boundary as plain data, not as identities.

blocks.code:
export const runtimeKey = '__loomRuntime'

export const runtimeVersion = '1'

export const runtimeSpecifiers: ReadonlyArray<string> = [
  'effect',
  '@athrio/loom-lang-services/LanguageService',
  'typescript',
]

blocks.markdown: `installHostRuntime` writes the host's live modules to the global, once, before the first service loads. Effect and the service contract are the host's own imports, inlined in its bundle. The TypeScript compiler is the editor's, handed in — the same `tsdk` the host analyzes the frame with — so a service checks the product against the user's TypeScript, never a second copy.

blocks.markdown:
The Loom language server resolves which languages a project has activated — built-in
modules and loaded `loom-service-<id>` packages — into one active set. The
`LanguageRegistry` indexes that set so the server finds a language by a
single lookup, not a scan: by `id` for a section's declared language, by extension
for a tangle path.

The registry draws on Effect — `HashMap` for its two indexes, `Option` for a
lookup that can miss — and on the `LanguageService` type it indexes.

blocks.code:
import { Context, Effect, HashMap, Layer, Option } from 'effect'
import type { LanguageService } from './LanguageService'

blocks.markdown:
`ActiveLanguages` holds the language set the server resolves from `loom.config.json`. It is empty until the server fills it — the registry over an empty set
simply finds nothing.

The registry builds two maps over the active set — one keyed by `id`, one by each
service's `extensions` — and returns an `Option` for each lookup: present when a
language claims that id or extension, absent otherwise. The `all` field keeps the full set for the
collect-and-register pass that hands every service's plugins to Volar.

blocks.markdown:
A language service is editor tooling, not a dependency of the code a project ships, so Loom keeps the services it installs in a store of its own and never writes to the consumer's `package.json`. This module owns that store: where it sits, which services it holds, and the two commands that fill and empty it.

`LanguageLoader` reads the store to load a project's active services. This module is the other half, the half that writes it. The two resolve the store's location through one shared function, so the loader always reads what `loom add` wrote.

blocks.title: The package a language ships in

blocks.markdown: Loom resolves an id to a package name by one rule: the id `python` names the package `@athrio/loom-service-python`. The id the author activates, the package the store installs, and the `id` the loaded service declares are one string from end to end, so finding a language's package is a substitution, not a lookup table to keep in step.

blocks.code:
export const servicePackage = (id: string): string =>
  `@athrio/loom-service-${id}`

blocks.markdown:
The store lives in the workspace, under `.loom/services/` — the directory `loom add` fills, beside the committed `config.yaml`. Git ignores the store, since Loom rebuilds it on demand, while the configuration is committed. Loom installs each language's service there once, to serve the whole workspace, and never under the user's home directory: a project's tools belong to the project, not to the machine.

`workspaceRoot` finds the workspace by walking up for the `.loom/` directory, and `storeDir` names the store `services` within it. The loader passes the directory holding a `.loom` file and the commands pass the working directory; either way the walk reaches the one workspace store, so a service the loader looks for is the service `loom add` placed. `LOOM_HOME` overrides the search with an explicit store — the hook a test or a shared setup uses to point every resolver at one directory. The configuration compiler reuses `workspaceRoot` to find the workspace whose `{Config}` sources it gathers.

`installedServices` lists the languages a store has installed, each by its id. It reads the `@athrio` scope of the store's `node_modules`, keeps the `loom-service-` packages, and strips the prefix back to the id — the inverse of `servicePackage`. A store that does not yet exist holds nothing.

`addService` installs a language's package into the store. It runs only when the store lacks the package, so adding an already-installed language is a quick no-op that needs no network.

When it must install, it prepares the store as a package root — a directory with a private `package.json` for the package manager to install into — and runs `npm install` with peers omitted. Loom's runtime is the reason for that flag: Effect, Volar's contracts, and the service interface itself are the service's peer dependencies, supplied by the host that loads it, so the install leaves them out and the store stays small.

An install that fails — `npm` absent, the registry unreachable, the package not published — fails with a `StoreError` carrying the cause, so the command that called it can report the reason rather than a stack trace.

`removeService` deletes a language's package from the store. It removes the package directory directly rather than asking the package manager to, so it undoes an install however the install arrived — and removing a language the store never held is harmless.

The store works in Effect: `Effect` runs each read and write, and `Data` gives `StoreError` its tag. Bun supplies the rest — `child_process` runs the package manager, which `promisify` turns into a promise `Effect.tryPromise` can await; `fs` reads the store's contents and adds and removes packages; `join` and `dirname` form and walk the store path up to the workspace root.

blocks.code:
import { Data, Effect } from 'effect'
import { execFile } from 'node:child_process'
import {
  existsSync,
  mkdirSync,
  readdirSync,
  rmSync,
  writeFileSync,
} from 'node:fs'
import { dirname, join } from 'node:path'
import { promisify } from 'node:util'

const run = promisify(execFile)
