blocks.markdown:
The `loom` command drives the rest, and its verbs are few. Each one names the thing it does, so the command line reads as the action it performs.

`loom init` sets up a workspace. It writes `conf.loom` and `.loom/`, and asks for nothing a sensible default can supply. From there the common path is short.

`loom add <language>` turns a language on: it installs that language's editor service into the store under `.loom/`, and records the language in `conf.loom`. `loom remove` takes both away again. It is `loom add typescript` that makes the greeting of the first chapter type-check. Nothing reaches your `package.json` — a Loom project need not be a JavaScript project at all — and nothing installs behind your back: open a file in a language you have not added, and the editor drops it to plain highlighting and offers the command that would add it. An install is something you ask for.

`loom run <file>` runs a loom without writing it to disk first. Composition happens in memory, the way a dev server compiles a component on the fly, so the program runs with nothing generated left in the tree. Writing files is a step for publishing, not a precondition for running.

`loom build` is that publishing step. It composes the corpus, writes the real source files, and bundles them to ship — and only here does Loom write out the configuration files it kept virtual, because the registry and the bundler that receive your package read them from disk. `loom tangle <path>` writes the composed files alone, for a build that wants that step by hand.

`loom weave` produces the documentation. Where tangle produces the code, weave produces the website — the prose and the code together, every section navigable, every anchor a link to what it names. It is the half of literate programming that faces the reader: the prose that tangle throws away is, in a weave, the product. The book you are reading is woven from Loom's own corpus.

Two commands round out the set. `loom status` shows what is active, what is installed, and where the store lives; `loom lsp` starts the language server the editor talks to, which you rarely type yourself.

Beneath the list is one standard the verbs hold to. Each names its own action, so the command reads as what it performs. The committed configuration is readable and the generated state is disposable, so you can always read the truth and throw the rest away. Every command that turns something on has the one that turns it off. And nothing that runs code happens by surprise.

Welcome. Loom is a literate programming framework. You write one file where prose and code live together, in the order that explains the program best, and Loom composes the code into real source files. This chapter is the short path through it: install the tool, write your first loom, tangle it to source, run it, and open it live in your editor.

> install the tool, write your first loom, tangle it to source, run it, and open it live in your editor

A demo note, living in the loom source — this is what a reader's annotation looks like. Resolve it by deleting the block.

Loom's command line is an npm package. Install it globally, and you have the `loom` command:

```sh
bun install -g @athrio/loom-cli
```

That one command is the whole tool — the tangler that writes your code, and the language server your editor talks to. It asks for Bun 1.2 or newer, and nothing else.

A loom is a `.loom` file. Open one, call it `greeting.loom`, and write a small program that greets the world:

```loom
---
Language: TypeScript
Package: src/main.ts
---

# A greeting

The `greet` function welcomes someone by name.

=>

export const greet = (name: string): string => `Hello, ${name}!`

# The entry point {Tangle}

The program greets the world and prints the result.

=>

::[A greeting]

console.log(greet("world"))
```

Read it top to bottom, the way a person would. Two marks carry the rhythm: `=>` turns prose into code, and `~` turns it back. The frontmatter at the top names the language the loom is written in and the file it writes. Below it, two sections tell the story in the order that reads best — what a greeting is, then the program that uses it.

The second section is where the composing happens. Its `{Tangle}` mark names it as the section written to the file, and `::[A greeting]` draws the first section in by name. So the greeting comes first, because it explains best, though in the finished file it lands where the program calls it. You compose by naming a section, never by copying it.

Tangling turns your loom into source. Run it:

```sh
loom tangle greeting.loom
```

Loom drops the prose, follows the anchor, and writes one file, `src/main.ts`:

```ts
export const greet = (name: string): string => `Hello, ${name}!`

console.log(greet("world"))
```

That is ordinary TypeScript — no prose, no trace of Loom, the two sections assembled in the order the program needs them. The file is the product; the loom is where it comes from, and where you keep working.

You need not write a file to run one. `loom run` composes the program in memory and runs it on the spot:

```sh
loom run greeting.loom
```

```
Hello, world!
```

Tangling to disk is a step for shipping, not a precondition for seeing your program work.

Open `greeting.loom` in your editor and it is alive, not a flat document. Loom highlights every section, follows `::[A greeting]` to the section it names when you ask for its definition, and flags a broken anchor or a malformed heading right where you wrote it. Set the project up — the next chapter's subject — and each language comes fully alive as well: the greeting is type-checked as you type, underlined in the loom rather than in a generated file you have never opened.

That is a first loom — one file you read like prose and run like code, with Loom keeping the two in step. The next chapter grows it into a project.

One loom grows into a project, and a project has a shape. A workspace is the directory you open to work in, and Loom marks one with a single committed file at its root, `conf.loom` — the sign that says Loom lives here, and the place its settings are kept. Beside it Loom generates a `.loom/` directory for what it can rebuild on demand: the language services it has installed, and its caches. You commit `conf.loom` and let Git ignore `.loom/`.

The workspace is the unit, not the package. One `conf.loom` governs the whole project — the languages it uses, the primary language a section falls back to when it names none, the marks that open an anchor. A monorepo does not scatter a config file into every package; it composes its packages through the book, and keeps one configuration above them all. The reason is the editor: it runs a single language server over the project, and that one server has to know every language any part of the project writes.

The configuration is itself a loom. A framework that asked everyone else to write literately and then configured itself in plain JSON would have broken its own premise, so `conf.loom` is prose beside the values it sets — each choice explained in the paragraph above it. And it is the single source of truth. The `package.json` a bundler expects, the `tsconfig.json` a type-checker expects, Loom composes from `conf.loom` and hands them over in memory, rather than leaving them in your tree to fall out of step. Configuration is virtual; only the installed services are real, because code that runs has to be a file on disk.

A loom, then, is not one file with its comments expanded. It is a narrative that composes a whole subsystem, and a subsystem is usually several files, often in several languages. One loom about a web feature might carry its markup in HTML, its behaviour in TypeScript, its manifest in JSON, and its deploy step in shell — each section in the language it belongs to, the prose explaining how they fit together. Tangle scatters the sections to the files they belong in; you keep the single story. That is the older and stronger form of literate programming: not one document to one module, but many files, many languages, one narrative.

At this stage we have no Loom-specific syntax at all. This chapter is the genesis of its own: it must name Loom's foundational primitives before it can be read. Loom is written in TypeScript with Effect, in pure functional style — but the syntax it defines is its own.

The very first thing that lets us materialise a Loom program is the *arrow*. Arrows have a simple and crucial role: they map *prose* to *code*.

blocks.code: // Hello! This is the beginning of the Loom language.

blocks.markdown:
Our very first program does nothing but show the rhythm of every Loom program. As you noticed, *tildes* `~` mark the transition back from code to prose, as the arrow marked the way in. A section holds any number of code and prose blocks, divided by the arrows and the tildes.

That is the whole rhythm. The primitives it rests on, gathered in one place:

1. Chapters `---`
2. Sections `#`
3. Prose
4. Code
5. Arrows `=>`
6. Tildes `~`
7. Sinks `[file]`
8. Specifiers `{Lang}`
9. Anchors `::[Name]`
10. Warps `{{ name = value }}`

The chapters that follow give each its shape — first the node every one of them is built from, then the tokens inside a line, the lines themselves, the sections they form, and the corpus above them.

`LoomAst` defines the container nodes — the tree the parse pipeline builds from a file's wefts. A `LoomDocument` holds one `LoomSection` per heading, and each section holds a `LoomHeading` plus its body wefts. The tree is flat: heading level is for the reader, never nesting.
A `LoomHeading` is a section's heading — the same parts the `HeadingWeft` carried: the start marker, an optional title, an optional language `specifier`, and an optional `sink`.

blocks.code:
export const LoomHeadingSchema = loomNode('LoomHeading', {
  headingStart: HeadingStartTokenSchema,
  title: Schema.optional(HeadingTitleTokenSchema),
  specifier: Schema.optional(SpecifierTokenSchema),
  sink: Schema.optional(SinkTokenSchema),
})
export type LoomHeading = typeof LoomHeadingSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: A `LoomSection` is one structural unit, opened by a heading at any level. Its body is two ordered runs of wefts: the `preamble` before the first transition, and the `code` after — the `=>` and `~` chunks in source order. A `{TOC}` section carries a third run, its `entries` — the toc wefts that list the book's parts and chapters. An ordinary section has none.

blocks.code:
export const LoomSectionSchema = loomNode('LoomSection', {
  heading: LoomHeadingSchema,
  preamble: Schema.Array(PreambleWeftSchema),
  code: Schema.Array(SectionBodyWeftSchema),
  entries: Schema.optional(Schema.Array(TocWeftSchema)),
})
export type LoomSection = typeof LoomSectionSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: A `LoomFrontmatter` is a chapter's opening block, parsed. It carries the membership line's `part`, `chapter`, and `title`, and the block's two fields: the `package` a `{Tangle}` section writes to, and the `language` its sections inherit. Every slot is optional, so a partial block still parses to a node the diagnostics can fault, and a file with no block has no frontmatter at all.

A `LoomDocument` is the root, the implicit module named by its file. Its `frontmatter` is the opening block, when the file has one; its `preamble` is the run of wefts between that block and the first heading — carrying any value Warps; and its `sections` are the flat list that follows. Every slot may be empty.
Each container is a `loomNode` built from Effect's `Schema`. The heading's subtokens come from `LoomTokens`; the preamble and body weft types come from `Weft`.

blocks.code:
import { Schema } from 'effect'
import { loomNode } from '#ast/LoomNode'
import {
  FrontmatterChapterTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterPartTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterPartNameTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterTitleTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterValueTokenSchema,
  HeadingStartTokenSchema,
  HeadingTitleTokenSchema,
  SinkTokenSchema,
  SpecifierTokenSchema,
} from './LoomTokens'
import {
  PreambleWeftSchema,
  SectionBodyWeftSchema,
  TocWeftSchema,
} from './Weft'

::[What this module draws on]

::[The heading]

::[The section]

::[The frontmatter]

::[The document]

blocks.markdown: `LoomTokens` defines the AST's leaf nodes — every span the parser recognises inside a line. Each token is built by `loomNode`, so it carries the same `type`, `position`, and `health` as any container, and most carry a `Probe`: the regular expression the classifier and tokeniser recognise it by. The compound tokens — `Specifier`, `Warp` — expose their parts as named subnodes, so a diagnostic can land on the exact part at fault.

A `Probe` is a regular expression annotated onto a token schema. The classifier and tokeniser recognise a token by its `Probe` match, then assemble the typed token from that match. `getProbe` reads the annotation back off a schema.

blocks.code:
export const Probe = 'loom/Probe'

export const getProbe = (schema: Schema.Top): Option.Option<RegExp> =>
  Option.fromNullishOr(schema.ast.annotations?.[Probe] as RegExp | undefined)

blocks.markdown: `HeadingStart` marks a heading line — one to six `#` and the mandatory space after. It is position-only: the marker text is its `source`, and the heading level is the hash count, recorded for the reader, since every heading makes a flat section.

A `Specifier` is `{Lang}`, a heading's language label — `{Bash}`, `{json}`, `{Prose}`. Its `open`, `label`, and `close` are separate subnodes, each with its own health, so a missing `}` lands on the `close`. The label's rule is health-aware: a real label matches `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+`, and an empty value is allowed only on a non-ok node — the placeholder an earlier pass leaves before the tokeniser fills it.

A `Sink` is the bracketed file a `{Tangle}` section writes to, written `[file]`. Brackets set a sink apart from a language `Specifier`, which wears braces. When a chapter's `Package` names a directory, each `{Tangle}` section marks the file it emits there — `[LoomNode.ts]` under `packages/loom-ast/src/ast/` — so the file travels with its section, not with the frontmatter. A sink exposes its `open`, `file`, and `close` as separate subnodes, each carrying its own health, so a diagnostic lands on the part at fault.

A chapter opens with a frontmatter block — a `---` fence, a run of field lines, and a closing `---` — that declares where the chapter belongs and what it produces. These tokens are its parts. `FrontmatterFence` is a fence line; its `Probe` is how the classifier finds it. A chapter always names itself, and its part is named once, by the chapter that opens it: that opener announces the part on its own line and its chapter on the next — `Part III: The shape of a loom`, then `Chapter 1: The node foundation`. A later chapter carries the part by its Roman numeral alone and names itself after the comma — `Part III, Chapter 3: Wefts`. `FrontmatterPart` holds the Roman numeral, `FrontmatterPartName` the part's name where the opener declares it, `FrontmatterChapter` the chapter's number, and `FrontmatterTitle` its title. Every other line is a `key: value` pair — a `FrontmatterKey`, either `Package` or `Language`, and the `FrontmatterValue` it binds. Only the fence carries a `Probe`; the tokeniser reads each remaining token from its line by position, the way it reads a heading's title.

The table of contents lists the book's parts and their chapters, and these tokens are its entries. A `TocPart` is a part's line — the `#### Part III — The shape of a loom` heading that opens a part in the listing. A `TocChapter` is a chapter's number and a `TocTitle` its name, both read from a numbered item like `5. The multi-file AST`: the list number is the chapter's `Chapter N`, and the text is its title. None carries a `Probe`. Like the frontmatter tokens, each is read from its line by position, once the classifier has marked the line as table-of-contents content.
`Arrow` (`=>`) opens a code chunk and `Tilde` (`~`) opens prose. Both are position-only — the marker is recognised here, the content after it elsewhere.

`HeadingTitle` is the human-readable title of a heading — the text between the marker and the first structural token, trimmed. It is position-only and computed from positions, not scanned, so it carries no `Probe`.

blocks.code:
export const HeadingTitleTokenSchema = loomNode('HeadingTitle', {})
export type HeadingTitleToken = typeof HeadingTitleTokenSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: `Code` is the content after `=>`, and `Prose` the content after `~`. Both are position-only; each one's `Probe` picks up the run of text following the marker.

A `Warp` is a declaration, `{{ name [: type] = value }}`, written in a preamble line. A `WarpAnchor` is a reference, `::[name]`, written in a code line. The two use different delimiters on purpose: a warp sits in prose, where `{{` is safe, but an anchor sits inside product code, where `{{` would collide with templating languages that own that syntax — `::[` does not. So a warp opens with `{{` and closes with `}}`, an anchor opens with `::[` and closes with `]`; both expose `open`, `close`, and `name` as subnodes. An anchor may also carry an optional `target` — a `(path)` after its close, the `.loom` file a directory anchor places as a chapter. The path is opaque here, for a later pass to resolve relative to the anchor's own file. An anchor may instead carry an optional `specifier` after its close — a `{Lang}` label or a `[sink]` an author wrote by mistake. A member names a chapter and never overrides, so a later pass faults any specifier an anchor wears; the grammar keeps it only to name that fault precisely rather than misread the trailing token as code. A declaration's `name` must be a TypeScript identifier; an anchor's `name` is looser — any text up to the closing delimiter, since it may name either a binding or a section by heading. A declaration also carries an optional `annotation` — the `: type` before the value — and a `default` — the `= value` it binds. Both are opaque text here; a later pass reads their structure and decides how the name binds.

An anchor's `open` and `close` are plain strings rather than fixed literals, because a package may set its own delimiter pair in `loom.json` when `::[` would collide with its product language. `AnchorDelims` is that pair, and `defaultAnchorDelims` holds the built-in `::[` and `]` — written as a concatenation so this corpus, which the tokeniser scans like any other, holds no bare `::[` of its own. For the same reason, this section writes the warp braces as the numeric character references `{` and `}`: a literal `{{` on a preamble line would tokenise as a Warp.

A package may choose its own anchor delimiter pair, so a bad pair must fail the way a mixed line terminator does: a parse-level error the editor and the tangler both report, never a silent misparse. `checkAnchorDelims` is that gate, and it raises a distinct, self-describing error for each fault. It rejects an empty delimiter, an open equal to its close, and whitespace inside either. It holds the open to more, since the open is what triggers anchor scanning: the open may not be one of Loom's own markers — the warp braces, the `=>` arrow, the `~` prose line, the `#` heading — nor a bracket or angle that pervades product code: `<`, `>`, `[`, or `]`. The close escapes that list, so the built-in `]` stays a valid close. Each error carries its own `message` naming the fault and suggesting a sound pair, so the recovery reads `emptyDocument(text, err.message)` and needs no separate message builder.
`LoomToken` is the union of every leaf token — the type the tokeniser emits.

blocks.code:
export const LoomTokenSchema = Schema.Union([
  HeadingStartTokenSchema,
  SpecifierTokenSchema,
  SinkTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterFenceTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterPartTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterPartNameTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterChapterTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterTitleTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterKeyTokenSchema,
  FrontmatterValueTokenSchema,
  TocPartTokenSchema,
  TocChapterTokenSchema,
  TocTitleTokenSchema,
  ArrowTokenSchema,
  TildeTokenSchema,
  HeadingTitleTokenSchema,
  CodeTokenSchema,
  ProseTokenSchema,
  WarpTokenSchema,
  WarpAnchorTokenSchema,
])
export type LoomToken = typeof LoomTokenSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: `LoomTokens` draws `Schema` for the token definitions, with `Option` wrapping the `Probe` annotation `getProbe` reads back off a schema. `Effect` and `Data` build the delimiter check and the tagged error it fails with. `loomNode` from the foundation gives each token its shared shape.

import { Data, Effect, Option, Schema } from 'effect'
import { loomNode } from '#ast/LoomNode'

blocks.markdown: `Weft` defines the line-level AST nodes. Each weft is one source line, typed by the mode it sits in and the tokens found on it. Wefts are health-bearing `loomNode`s, so they carry `type`, `position`, and `health` like any node. A weft's position spans exactly one line. `LoomWeft`, the union of them all, is the element the tokeniser streams between passes.

A `HeadingWeft` is a `#`-heading line. Its `headingStart` records the level for the reader, and `title` is the optional trimmed text after the marker — the title names the section, normalised to an identifier. The `specifier` and the `sink` are optional too: the specifier is the language label `{Lang}`, the sink is the bracketed file `[file]`, and a heading may carry either, both, or neither. The classifier emits the weft with incomplete placeholders for the subtokens it has not recognised, and the tokeniser settles them.

blocks.code:
export const HeadingWeftSchema = loomNode('HeadingWeft', {
  headingStart: HeadingStartTokenSchema,
  title: Schema.optional(HeadingTitleTokenSchema),
  specifier: Schema.optional(SpecifierTokenSchema),
  sink: Schema.optional(SinkTokenSchema),
})
export type HeadingWeft = typeof HeadingWeftSchema.Type

blocks.markdown:
The five body wefts are the lines inside a section. An `ArrowWeft` is the `=>` line that opens code, carrying any inline `code` and the anchors on it. A `CodeWeft` is a later line in code mode — opaque to Loom, but its anchors are recognised. A `TildeWeft` is the `~` line that opens prose, carrying any inline `prose` and its anchors. A `ProseWeft` is a later line in prose mode — opaque too, but its anchors are recognised. A `PreambleWeft` is a default-mode line before any transition, carrying the warp declarations and any anchors on it.

Every body weft carries its `anchors`, because an anchor is read in code and in prose alike. A code anchor composes the named section; a prose anchor places a chapter or merely links to it. A later pass reads which plane an anchor takes from the weft it sits on.

A `FrontmatterWeft` is one line of the frontmatter block — a fence, a `Part` or `Chapter` line, or a `key: value` field. One weft kind covers them all, each filling the slots its line owns: a fence line fills `fence`; a `Part` line fills `part` and `partName`; a `Chapter` line fills `chapter` and `title`; the joined `Part …, Chapter …` line fills `part`, `chapter`, and `title`; a `Package` or `Language` line fills `key` and `value`. This mirrors the heading weft, which carries its title, specifier, and sink in the same optional way. The document builder gathers a file's frontmatter wefts into one `LoomFrontmatter`.

blocks.code:
export const FrontmatterWeftSchema = loomNode('FrontmatterWeft', {
  fence: Schema.optional(FrontmatterFenceTokenSchema),
  part: Schema.optional(FrontmatterPartTokenSchema),
  partName: Schema.optional(FrontmatterPartNameTokenSchema),
  chapter: Schema.optional(FrontmatterChapterTokenSchema),
  title: Schema.optional(FrontmatterTitleTokenSchema),
  key: Schema.optional(FrontmatterKeyTokenSchema),
  value: Schema.optional(FrontmatterValueTokenSchema),
})
export type FrontmatterWeft = typeof FrontmatterWeftSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: A `TocWeft` is one line inside a `{TOC}` section: a part heading, a chapter entry, or a blank spacer. One weft kind covers them all, each filling the slots its line owns — a part line fills `part`; a chapter line fills `chapter` and `title`; a blank line fills none. This mirrors the frontmatter weft, which carries its own line's parts in the same optional way. The classifier marks each line of the contents as a `TocWeft` so a part's `####` heading never opens a section and a chapter's list item never reads as prose, and the tokeniser settles the slots. The document builder gathers a `{TOC}` section's toc wefts as its entries.

`LoomWeft` is the union of every weft kind — the tokeniser's stream element. `SectionBodyWeft` is the narrower union the grammar admits inside a section body: arrow, code, tilde, and prose, in the forward-only order the classifier enforces.

blocks.code:
export const LoomWeftSchema = Schema.Union([
  HeadingWeftSchema,
  ArrowWeftSchema,
  TildeWeftSchema,
  PreambleWeftSchema,
  ProseWeftSchema,
  CodeWeftSchema,
  FrontmatterWeftSchema,
  TocWeftSchema,
])
export type LoomWeft = typeof LoomWeftSchema.Type

export const SectionBodyWeftSchema = Schema.Union([
  ArrowWeftSchema,
  CodeWeftSchema,
  TildeWeftSchema,
  ProseWeftSchema,
])
export type SectionBodyWeft = typeof SectionBodyWeftSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: Each weft is a `loomNode` built from Effect's `Schema`; its subtokens are the heading, arrow, tilde, code, prose, specifier, sink, warp, and anchor schemas from `LoomTokens`.

blocks.markdown: Every node in the Loom AST — a container like a section or a leaf like a token — shares one shape and is built by one combinator. `LoomNode` is that foundation: it defines source positions, the health and diagnostic vocabulary every node carries, and `loomNode`, which stamps the shared `type`, `position`, `source`, and `health` fields onto each node schema.
Every node type is an Effect `Schema.Struct`, so one definition both validates a node and gives it a type.

blocks.code: import { Schema } from 'effect'

blocks.markdown: A `Position` is the span a node covers — a `start` and `end` `Point`. A `Point` carries a `line`, an optional `column`, and an `offset`. The `offset` is the byte index Volar maps against; line and column only serve diagnostics.
A `Diagnostic` is a positioned message with a `Severity` — `error`, `warning`, or `info`. `info` is not a failure: a node carrying only `info` diagnostics stays healthy.

Every node carries `Health`: a `status` and the diagnostics behind it. `ok` is final and clean; `error` and `warning` describe problems; `incomplete` marks a node an earlier pass left for a later one to finish. `okHealth` and `incompleteHealth` are the shared constants for the two cases with no diagnostics.

An `UnexpectedToken` is a stray fragment the schema rejected — an orphan bracket, a duplicate tag — kept with its position and text rather than dropped. It carries no health of its own: its presence in a node's `unexpected` array is the anomaly, and the parent node's health reflects it.

`loomNode` builds a node schema from a tag and the node's own fields. To every node it adds the shared four — a `type` literal that discriminates the union, the `position`, the `source` slice the node covers, and its `health` — plus an optional `unexpected` array. A walker then recognises any node by its `type` and recurses into any field that has one.

blocks.code:
export const loomNode = <
  Tag extends string,
  Fields extends Schema.Struct.Fields,
>(
  tag: Tag,
  fields: Fields,
) =>
  Schema.Struct({
    type: Schema.tag(tag),
    position: PositionSchema,
    source: Schema.String,
    health: HealthSchema,
    unexpected: Schema.optional(Schema.Array(UnexpectedTokenSchema)),
    ...fields,
  })

::[Built on Effect Schema]

blocks.markdown: `LoomCorpusAst` is the multi-file AST — pure data, no pipeline (that is `LoomCorpusAstBuilder`). Every loaded `.loom` is a `LoomModule` keyed by its path, holding its source, parsed `doc`, its composed `product`, and import edges. The corpus is just a map of those modules. The `product` is what the product pass builds for each module; a cross-file reference stays an edge there, resolved later at projection by `fromProduct`.

A `Path` is a resolved `.loom` path, a module's identity. A `LoomModule` carries one file's `product` beside its `text` and parsed `doc`. The `product` is the module's composition graph, built straight from the parse, so every loaded module carries one. A `LoomCorpusAst` is the map of modules by path.

A `Modules` map keys each loom in a corpus by its path. The corpus is one map; the graph over it is the anchor graph, where a section in one loom composes a section in another. The forward reach a run and a tangle scope to, and the reverse reach an edit reprojects, both walk that graph, so both live beside it.

blocks.code: type Modules = ReadonlyMap<Path, LoomModule>

blocks.markdown:
Names resolve here — the lexical lookups that turn an `::[Name]` anchor into the section it points at. A `SectionRef` locates that section: the module it lives in, and the section itself.

An anchor names a section by its raw title, and resolves that title in one file. A bare `::[Name]` resolves in the anchor's own file. A `::[Name](path.loom)` resolves in the file the path names, taken relative to the anchor's own. So a name is lexical, not a corpus-wide symbol, and two files reuse a title freely.

`titlesIn` indexes one file's titled sections by their raw title, the first of any in-file duplicate winning — the duplicate itself a fault the diagnostics raise. `titleIndexes` builds that index for every file, once. `resolveAnchor` is the lookup: it picks the target file — the anchor's own, or the one its path names — and finds the title there. `resolveRelative` resolves that path against the anchor's own directory, the anchor's path minus its last segment, folding `.` and `..` as it goes.
`anchorsOf` gathers every anchor a section carries — across its preamble and its body, code and prose alike — since the reachability walk and the navigation read the same set.

blocks.code:
const anchorsOf = (section: LoomSection): ReadonlyArray<WarpAnchorToken> =>
  pipe(
    [...section.preamble, ...section.code],
    Array.flatMap((weft) => weft.anchors),
  )

blocks.markdown:
A run and a tangle need only part of the corpus: the entry and the sections it composes. `reachable` is the forward reach over the anchor graph — the entry, every module a section in it names through an `::[…]` anchor, and the modules those name in turn. Tangling one file never composes an unrelated loom, and a loom the entry never anchors never fails its build. `transitiveDependents` is the reverse — every module that composes a section in a given file, transitively — so an edit reprojects each file that inlined it.

`reach` grows a set along one direction of the edges until it adds nothing more, guarding a cycle so the walk ends. `anchorEdges` reads the corpus once: each anchor a module carries becomes an edge to the module that holds the section it names.

An anchor reaches a section, and a warp anchor reaches the value warp it names — the editor follows both, and `symbolAt` decides which. It reads the kind under a cursor from `LoomSymbol`'s table, so a section anchor resolves a title across the corpus while a warp anchor resolves a name within its own section, each verb dispatching on the same classifier the mappings and colours read. A `CorpusLocation` is where a step lands: the module and the span, in the source's own offsets, which the editor turns into its own coordinates.

blocks.code: export type CorpusLocation = { readonly path: Path; readonly position: Position }

blocks.markdown: Each step starts from the node under a cursor. `anchorAt` finds the anchor whose `::[…]` span covers a source offset. `titledSectionAt` finds the heading whose title covers it, and `sectionRefAt` pairs that section with its module.

A resolved section then locates its heading and the anchors that reach it. `titleLocation` points at a section's heading title. `targetAt` reads the section under a cursor — the heading it sits in, or the section an anchor under it resolves to. `anchorsResolvingTo` gathers every anchor across the corpus that resolves to a given section, each by the span a caller picks: the whole anchor for a reference, its name alone for a rename.

`sectionDefinitionAt` follows a section anchor to the heading it names — in the anchor's own file, or the file its path names. `sectionReferencesAt` reads the section under a cursor, a heading or a resolved anchor either way, and returns that heading beside every anchor across the corpus that resolves to it. The exported verbs call these once `symbolAt` names the cursor a section.

A warp anchor resolves within its section, not across the corpus. `::[ratio]` names the value warp `ratio` its section declares, or the document's when the section declares none — the same scope `symbolsOf` classifies a warp anchor by, so the editor and the classifier agree. `valueWarpsIn` reads a preamble's value warps, the lang warp excepted. `sectionCovering` gives the section an offset sits in, and `warpDefFor` picks the warp a name sees there: the section's own, else the document's, so a section warp shadows the document's. `warpNameAt` reads the name under a cursor, whether a `{{ratio = …}}` definition or a `::[ratio]` anchor. `warpBindingAt` ties them together — the definition and every anchor that binds to it — and `warpDefLocation` and `warpReferences` shape that binding into the definition span and the reference spans a caller picks.

A table-of-contents entry resolves differently from an anchor: it names a chapter by title, across the whole corpus, not a section within one file. A chapter's identity is its frontmatter, not its heading — a heading can differ from the title the book lists, or be missing. `chapterTitleToken` reads that identity: the frontmatter `title`, and the first heading only for a loom that carries no frontmatter. `chapterIndex` maps the title to where the chapter declares it, the first of any duplicate winning. `tocEntryTitleAt` reads the entry title under a cursor and `frontmatterTitleAt` the title in a chapter's own frontmatter; `chapterNameAt` takes either, so a cursor on the entry and a cursor on the chapter reach the same name. `chapterDefinitionAt` follows that name to the chapter the index holds. `unresolvedTocEntriesIn` is the other side: every entry whose title names no chapter, for the diagnostics to fault. `tocNameGroup` gathers the whole name — the chapter's own title and every entry that names it — so references and rename move together, and `chapterGroupAt` reads that group from the cursor. A part resolves the same way, keyed by its number rather than its title. A part is announced once, in its opening chapter's standalone `Part R: name`, whose `partName` is the part's identity; `partOpenerIndex` maps each part number to that announcement. The number under a cursor comes from a chapter's `Part R` through `frontmatterPartNumberAt`, or from a contents part heading through `tocPartNumberAt`, which reads it out of the `Part R — name` label; `partNumberAt` takes either. `partDefinitionAt` follows the number to the opener, and `partGroup` gathers the whole part — the announcement, every chapter's `Part R`, and every contents heading that names it — which `partGroupAt` reads from the cursor.

`definitionAt` follows the reference under a cursor to its definition — a section anchor to its heading, a warp anchor to its warp, a contents entry or a chapter's own title to that chapter, and a `Part R` or a contents part heading to where the part is announced. `referencesAt` returns every place the thing under the cursor is named — a section and its anchors, a warp and its anchors, a chapter's title and the entries that name it, or a part's announcement and every `Part R` and contents heading that names it. Each reads the kind from `symbolAt` and dispatches on it through one `Match`.

A rename is one edit across the scope a name spans: a section's title and every anchor that resolves to it, a warp's definition and every anchor that binds to it, or a chapter's frontmatter title and every contents entry that names it, move together so the name and its references never drift. `sectionRenameAt` gathers a section's spans; the exported `renameAt` reads `symbolAt`, sending a warp to `warpReferences` and a chapter to `chapterGroupAt`. Each span is a name alone — a title, or the name inside a `::[…]` — so the brackets and any `(path)` stay put. A part is not renamed here yet: its name is embedded in the contents label and its chapter references are bare numbers, so a clean rename waits on the tokeniser splitting that label into its number and name.

`navigationRangeAt` is the span a navigable symbol occupies — the whole title, anchor, warp, or `Part R`, read from the same classification the mappings and colours read. It is the range a definition link underlines on hover and the range a rename box covers, so the editor takes the whole name rather than the one word the cursor sits in. The rule is the capability: a kind whose `profileOf` sets `navigation` spans its whole token, and everything that draws a selection reads it here. `renameRangeAt` narrows that to the renameable — the span only where `renameAt` has edits to make — so a part, navigable but not yet renameable, underlines on hover yet opens no rename box.

Every node carries a health, and a health holds diagnostics. `diagnosticsIn` is the walk that reads them out. It descends any node, gathers the diagnostics it carries, and skips the `health` field itself, so a node's diagnostics are collected once rather than again at every child. It keeps every severity — error, warning, and informational note alike — because the editor surfaces them all.

`moduleDiagnostics` is the per-file view: one module's diagnostics from both tiers, the grammatical health on its parsed document and the semantic health on its product. An anchor that resolves to nothing or crosses a language carries its fault on the `NameRef` the product pass built, so the walk reads the `product.code` alone; a tangle sink's `File` repeats that same `Code`, and reading it too would count every sink's diagnostics twice. This is what the editor reads for an open `.loom`.

`corpusErrors` is the whole-corpus view the tangler takes, narrowed to errors. It keeps each module's error-severity diagnostics and returns every failing module with them, so the tangler refuses a broken build with one report rather than failing at the first bad file. A warning never blocks a tangle; only an error does.

Effect supplies `Schema` for the model, `Array`, `Option`, and `pipe` for the queries, and `Match` for the navigation's per-kind dispatch. The module bundles the parse model `LoomDocument` and the product model `ProductSchema`, with `LoomSection`, the unit the corpus walks, the `WarpAnchorToken` an anchor is, the `WarpToken` a value warp is defined by, the `TocWeft` a contents line is — with the `TocTitleToken` it names a chapter and the `TocPartToken` it names a part — and `Diagnostic` and `Position`, what a corpus walk gathers and a diagnostic lands on. `symbolAt` and `profileOf` from `LoomSymbol` name the token under a cursor and read its features, so the navigation reads the same table the mappings and colours do.

blocks.markdown:
Loom's pipeline is a chain of small passes between explicit models. This module
is one of them, and it is the one that composes: it folds a parsed `.loom`
document — a `LoomDocument` of sections — straight into its `Product`, the
author's own code with each `::[…]` anchor turned into an edge. A section's name
anchors already form the document's composition
graph, so the pass reads that graph off the parse and builds the product from it,
rather than generating a program to compute the same result.

The pass is a build, the same shape as the parser before it. The parser folds a
stream of wefts into the Loom AST; this folds that AST's sections into the product.
It is total by construction. Every section becomes one `Code`, and a section
whose sink names a file becomes a `File` as well. An anchor that resolves to
nothing becomes an empty `Fragment` carrying error health on its own node — a
fault the editor reports, never a thrown exception. Grammatical health, a
malformed anchor say, is already on the parse; semantic health, an anchor that
resolves to nothing or crosses a language, is attached here, where the pass has
the names to judge it.

What an anchor edge carries is an identity, not a copy. A `NameRef` records
the `SectionId` its anchor resolves to and nothing more; `fromProduct` follows
that edge across the corpus and inlines the target later, at projection. So this
pass stays within one document: it resolves each anchor against that document's
own sections and leaves the cross-file inlining to the projection that reads the
whole corpus.

The pass reads the Loom AST and writes the product AST, so it draws from both. From
`effect` come the array and option combinators, `Match` for dispatching on a
weft, `Order` for sorting a run's anchors by position, and `pipe` for the spine.
From the Loom side come the document and section types, the warp and anchor
tokens a section carries, and the section-body weft a code run is made of. The
fault catalog gives `faulty` and the three anchor faults this pass raises, and
the tokeniser's `normaliseTitle` folds a heading into the name that identifies
its section. From the product side come the schema constructors for every node the
pass builds — the `Code` of a section, its `Fragment` and `NameRef` leaves, and
the `File` a `{Tangle}` section emits — together with the `SectionId` that stamps
each one.

blocks.markdown:
`ProductBuilder` is the service the rest of the pipeline calls. Its one method,
`build`, wraps the pure `buildProduct` in an Effect so callers compose it like any
other pass. It takes the document's file path alongside the document, because each
section's identity is its path and name, and the path is known only to the caller.
It also takes the package's primary language, the default a section inherits when
the frontmatter declares no `Language`, which the caller resolves from the file's
configuration.

`buildProduct` is the spine, and it runs from the document's head to its tail. It
resolves the primary language and the output package from the frontmatter, indexes
the sections by the name each anchor reaches them by, then maps every section to a
`Built` — its `Code`, and the `File` a `{Tangle}` section or a tangle sink adds —
and folds those into the module's `Product`.

A name anchor reaches a section by its heading title — the one handle every
section has. The index is keyed by that title, and each title maps to the section
the title names: its title normalised to an identifier.

A title can repeat, and the index keeps every section that claims one rather than
letting the last overwrite the rest. So a title maps to a *list* of sections, not
a single one. A list of one is a clean resolution; a longer list is an ambiguity
the anchor reports rather than guesses through.

The primary language is the one a specifier-less section inherits, resolved from
three sources in order. The frontmatter's `Language` field sets it, lowercased to a
language id. The field is optional: when the frontmatter omits it, the language
falls back to the package's configured language, passed in as `packageLanguage`,
and then to `plaintext` when the package configures none. `frontmatterLanguage`
reads the field and yields nothing when it is absent, so the fallback chain walks
the three sources in turn.

A tangle sink resolves its language a fourth way: it carries no specifier, only a
path, so `extensionLanguage` reads the language off the path's extension. It maps
`.ts` and its siblings to `typescript`, `.tsx` to `tsx`, the JavaScript extensions
to `javascript` and `jsx`, and `.json` to `json`. An unlisted extension falls back
to itself, so a new language reads as its bare extension until a service claims it.
A `.json` sink composes as JSON even in a TypeScript document.

`sectionLanguage` resolves any section's language by these rules — its specifier,
a sink's extension, or the document default — with one reserved specifier:
`{Config}` is the workspace or package configuration, whose body is YAML, so it
reads as YAML even though Loom loads the configuration from the compiled
`.loom/config.yaml` rather than this section. `nameIndex` records the language
beside each title, so when an anchor binds, the pass compares the composing
section's language to the section it reaches and marks a cross-language
transclusion when they differ.

A warp binds a name its section's anchors compose. A value warp binds the name to
a literal — a string, a number — and `::[n]` substitutes that literal's value where
the anchor stood. A value needs no edge and no section: the pass drops the value in
place. A quoted string yields its content — `{{kw = "const"}}` lands `const` — and
the author writes any surrounding syntax, the quotes for a string slot, around the
anchor itself.

`valueWarps` gathers them. It keeps each warp that carries a value, mapped to its
decoded literal and its position. `decodeLiteral` unwraps a quoted string to its
content and leaves a bare number as written, so the decode never evaluates an
expression. A warp with no value binds nothing — a fault the tokeniser already
raised. The document's `lang` warp carries an annotation rather than a value, so it
never enters the map.

A section's heading decides what it contributes. Every section composes to one
`Code`: its identity, its language, and its fragments in composition order. A
`{Tangle}` section contributes a `File` on top — the same `Code` paired with the
frontmatter `Package` path, and the `[file]` tag appended when the package names a
directory.

A `Built` is the pair a section yields: its `Code`, which always lands in the
product, and the `File` a `{Tangle}` section or a tangle sink adds, which an
ordinary section leaves absent. `buildSection` gathers the section's value warps,
resolves its language and identity, and composes its code.

`tangleFile` decides a section's file. A `{Tangle}` section writes to the package
path, so it emits a `File` there when the frontmatter names a package; any other
section emits nothing. `tangleFilePath` appends the `[file]` tag to a directory
package, and passes a file package through unchanged.

`buildCode` turns a section's code into one `Code`. It walks each code run, binding
each anchor against the value warps and the name index, and gathers the fragments
and edges the runs yield. The `Code` carries the section's identity and language;
a section with no code composes an empty one.

A *code piece* is the unit of composition: one line from a code weft, or the inline
code after an arrow, extended to the line's end so its trailing newline is kept.
`pieceOf` lifts a weft to a piece, and a prose weft yields none.

A run sheds its blank lines at the edges but keeps them inside. The section's last
run trims both edges, since it closes the section; every earlier run keeps a single
trailing blank as its seam to the next code chunk. So a section that interleaves
code and prose composes with one blank line at each seam, never a doubled gap.
`codeRuns` splits the section into these runs, one per `=>` arrow, and discards the
prose between them.

`walkRun` turns one run into its parts. The run's anchors, sorted by position,
partition its text: each anchor contributes the fragment before it and then its
edge, and a closing fragment runs to the end. Each fragment carries its `.loom`
span as the origin it maps back to, and the text is the source slice itself — no
escaping, since a fragment is data the projection reads, not a literal it must
quote. Each boundary is computed from the anchor's index, so no cursor threads
through the text.

`bindAnchor` turns one anchor into one part. A value warp composes its literal, a
named section composes an edge to that section, and an anchor that resolves to
nothing composes an empty fragment in its place. So a broken or unresolvable anchor
never leaves a dangling reference: the section always composes, and the product code
around the anchor keeps its language service.

A resolved anchor takes one of two paths. When the name is a value warp, the anchor
composes that warp's text as a `Fragment` landing where the anchor stood — no edge,
since a literal reaches no section. Otherwise the name is looked up among the
section titles. One match resolves to a `NameRef`, the edge that carries the target
section's identity; the projection follows it and inlines the section there. A match
in another language is a cross-language transclusion: the edge stands, since the
section is real, but the pass marks the anchor, because a JSON section composed into
a TypeScript one shares no file.

An anchor that cannot resolve leaves an empty fragment instead, and what rides on
that fragment depends on why it failed. A grammatically broken anchor — an empty
`::[]` or an unclosed `::[n` — already carries the parser's fault on its own span,
so the fragment adds none. A well-formed name that matches no section is unresolved;
one that matches more than one is ambiguous. The fragment carries that diagnostic
and lands it on the anchor.

blocks.title: A section's name and its spans

blocks.markdown:
A section's name is its heading title normalised to an identifier, through the
tokeniser's `normaliseTitle` — `Multiplier Function` becomes `MultiplierFunction`.
Every heading has a title, so the function is total, and the name pairs with the
module path to make the section's corpus-wide identity.

The span helpers read a run's source and bounds. `sourceOf` joins a piece list's
source, `spanOf` takes the span from its first and last positions, and `between`
pairs two points into a span.

The tangle composes the sections in the source's own order — the spine first, then
the helpers it reaches for, each section named by its title, the local anchor form
Loom prefers.

blocks.markdown:
`ProductAst` is the shape of the product — the author's code, with each transclusion expressed as an edge rather than copied. Its unit is the `Code` of one section. The product pass builds one `Code` per section, and `fromProduct` later flattens a section to text and mappings, following its edges across the corpus.

An edge holds a target key, not a pointer. So the schema stays flat and serialisable even though the graph it describes recurses — and may hold a cycle, which `fromProduct` cuts.

Code is one of two channels. Prose is its peer, a `WovenProse` woven from the same fragments. A tangle section returns neither: it binds a composed result to a file path as a `File`, the descriptor the tangler writes.

`productNode` builds each product node: it stamps a `type` discriminator and a defaulted `health` onto the fields it wraps. There is no render-order annotation, since the product is flattened by a graph walk rather than by emitting fields in order.

A `SectionId` is a node's corpus-wide identity: the `.loom` `path` and the section `name` within it. Two files may each define a `Main`, so identity is module-qualified. `keyOf` JSON-encodes the pair into the key `fromProduct` uses for its cycle-cutting visited set.

A `Fragment` is a literal product span — the code the author wrote — with the `.loom` `origin` it maps 1:1 back to.

A `Ref` is a transclusion edge. It records a name anchor — `::[Heading]` naming a section in the same document — so the projection composes its target into the referring section's namespace, and the two share one scope. A `Ref` carries two fields: a `target`, the `Option<SectionId>` the edge points at, `None` when the anchor resolved to nothing; and an `anchor`, the `::[…]` site in the consuming section, where a resolution diagnostic lands. `Ref` is the single edge kind, named `NameRef`.

A `Code` is one section, composed: its `origin` identity, its product `languageId`, and its `fragments` in composition order — a transcluded section before the code that uses it. Each fragment is a `Fragment`, a literal span, or a `Ref`, a transclusion edge; `Part` is the union of the two. The product pass returns one `Code` per section, keyed by name.

A `WovenProse` is the prose channel's `Code`. It carries the same `origin` and the same `fragments`, so one `Part` type serves both channels. A fragment is a span of the section's prose, and a reference transcludes another section's prose. The channel a fragment belongs to is the field that holds it, never the fragment itself. Prose is always Markdown, so a `WovenProse` declares no `languageId`.

A `File` is what a tangle section composes to: a file `path` paired with the `Code` bound to it. It is a value, not an effect — the tangler flattens the `code` across the corpus and performs the write, so building a `File` touches no disk.

`Product` is a module's whole composition — the graph the product pass produces for one `.loom`. A module has many roots, so a `Product` holds many nodes: its `code`, one `Code` per ordinary section, and its `files`, one `File` per tangle sink. The edges are the refs those nodes carry. A node no same-module ref folds in is a root; the rest are members, reached only through an edge.

The graph is what `compile` resolves. It reads a `Product`, takes the roots, and flattens each by following its edges across the corpus. So a `Product` is left unresolved here — it carries the whole graph, not the flattened roots — and resolving it is the next pass's work, not this structure's. Prose is no part of it: the narration is read from the parse, in document order, never composed here.
Effect's `Schema` defines the nodes, and `Effect` fills the health default. `Health`, `okHealth`, and `Position` come from the foundation, giving each node its health and its source spans.

blocks.markdown:
A project authors its configuration in a single `{Config}` section, and `@athrio/loom-config` reads it from a compiled file, `.loom/config.yaml`. `ManifestBuilder` is the pass that compiles the one from the other. It searches a workspace for the `{Config}` source, decodes it, and writes the resulting `WorkspaceManifest` through the configuration service.

The pass lives here, in the language compiler, rather than in `@athrio/loom-config`, because it parses `.loom` files. Parsing pulls in the compiler, and the compiler already depends on the configuration package; compiling the manifest here, and leaving the package to read the result, keeps that dependency pointing one way.

`loomFiles` walks a workspace and returns every `.loom` file under it. It descends each directory but skips the ones that hold no source: the generated `.loom/` store, the `node_modules` and `dist` build outputs, and the `.git` directory. A `{Config}` source can sit in any of the files it returns, since the configuration is marked by a section's specifier, not by a filename.

`configBodyOf` finds a document's `{Config}` section and returns its body. A `{Config}` section is one whose heading specifier is the language-style label `Config`. `codeOf` pulls the code from one chunk — a code weft's line, or the inline code that follows an `=>` — and yields nothing for a marker. `configBodyOf` joins those chunks into the YAML text the configuration service decodes, and returns nothing for a document with no such section.

`diskSource` reads a file's text for the parser, failing with the parser's own `ReadError` when the file cannot be read — the parser then degrades that file to an empty document, and the pass skips it.

`buildManifest` turns the workspace configuration it found into the manifest the configuration package reads, carrying the same fields across — the languages, the corpus directory, the primary, the anchor, and the settings. A search that found no configuration yields an empty manifest, every field absent.

`ManifestBuilder` exposes two steps. Its `build` searches a workspace for the `{Config}` source and shapes it into a manifest, parsing each candidate file through the corpus builder and taking the first that carries a configuration. Its `materialize` runs `build` and writes the result through `LoomConfig`, the single call that produces a workspace's `.loom/config.yaml`. The CLI's activation commands call `materialize` after they edit the `{Config}` source, so the compiled file stays in step with what a person authored.

Effect supplies the effect type and the service primitive, with `Match`, `Option`, and `Array` for pulling the code from a section's chunks. Bun's synchronous filesystem reads each file and lists each directory; its path module resolves the files it descends into. From `@athrio/loom-config` come the configuration service, the `parseConfig` decoder, and the `WorkspaceConfig` and `WorkspaceManifest` shapes the pass reads and writes. The corpus builder parses each `.loom` file, with its `Source` seam and `ReadError`, and the AST supplies the `LoomDocument` the pass reads, the `SectionBodyWeft` chunks it pulls code from, and the `Path` it works in.

blocks.markdown: `LoomMemo` is the incremental build cache — the reason editing one `.loom` does not recompile the project. Building a module — parsing it, then composing its product — is the costly half; projecting it afterwards is cheap. So a keystroke stays cheap by not rebuilding what did not change: each module is built once, kept by path, and returned on the next request. It is deliberately just a `path → LoomModule` cache — it knows nothing of imports, so which paths an edit invalidates stays the compiler's call, handed to `evict`.

`MemoStats` is the window onto the cache: hits against misses is the work an edit avoided, and size is how many modules are kept. The state holds the kept modules and the running tallies immutably behind a `SynchronizedRef`, and `empty` is its starting value.

`LoomMemo` is that cache as a service — one instance is the project's build cache. `get` is a hit (return the kept module) or a miss (run `build`, keep it, count it), checked and built in one atomic step so a module builds at most once under concurrent gets. The `build` it runs may itself `get` the cache for its imports. `entries` exposes the kept modules for the compiler to project and to walk. `evict` forgets a set of paths by copy-and-change, never mutating the kept map. `stats` reports the counters.

`get` caches each module whole — its parse and its `product` together — since `LoomCorpusAstBuilder` builds both in one pass. `fill` keeps the cache consistent with a full build: after running the whole corpus, the compiler hands `fill` the produced modules, and each overwrites its kept entry. `fill` updates only paths still kept, so an edit that evicted a module in the meantime never resurrects it.
Effect supplies `SynchronizedRef` for the atomic state, and `Array`, `Option`, and `pipe` for the operations. `LoomModule` and `Path` from the corpus model are what it caches and keys by.

blocks.markdown:
`@athrio/loom-config` reads a Loom project's configuration. A project keeps it in one compiled file, `.loom/config.yaml`, under the workspace's generated store — the activation commands write it, and a project may also compile it from literate `{Config}` sources. This package reads that file and answers, for any `.loom` file, the configuration that applies to it.

It reads the compiled file rather than the `{Config}` source for one reason: it is a leaf. Reading the source would mean parsing a `.loom` file, which would pull in the language compiler, and the compiler already depends on this package. The compiled `.loom/config.yaml` breaks that cycle. The pass that compiles the `{Config}` sources runs inside the compiler, where the parser already lives, and writes the file this package reads.

The read is synchronous. The editor resolves a file's configuration inside Volar's synchronous projection hook, which cannot wait for a promise, so the resolver reads through Bun's synchronous filesystem. A missing or malformed file resolves to the empty configuration rather than failing, so a project still opens when its configuration is absent or broken.

blocks.title: The pieces a configuration is built from

blocks.markdown: Three small shapes recur across every configuration. The anchor pair holds the delimiters a section's anchors open and close with, each side optional so a configuration overrides only the one it cares about. A language entry carries the one thing activating a language can override — the `service` package that gives it editor support — and is otherwise empty, since a language's presence is what activates it. The settings map holds, for each language id, a free-form bag the host passes to that language's service untouched.

A project has one configuration, authored in a single `{Config}` section. It names the languages the project activates — one set for the whole project, because the editor runs a single language server over it — and sets the defaults every section inherits: the primary language a specifier-less section falls back to, the anchor delimiters, the per-language settings, and the name of the corpus directory looms sit in. A package declares no configuration of its own; a book composes the package layout literately, in its higher-order sinks, rather than in the configuration.

blocks.code:
export const WorkspaceConfigSchema = Schema.Struct({
  languages: Schema.Record(Schema.String, LanguageSchema),
  corpus: Schema.optional(Schema.String),
  primary: Schema.optional(Schema.String),
  anchor: Schema.optional(AnchorSchema),
  settings: Schema.optional(SettingsSchema),
})

export type WorkspaceConfig = typeof WorkspaceConfigSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: `parseConfig` reads the YAML body of a `{Config}` section and decodes it into the workspace configuration. It parses the text as YAML and validates the result against `WorkspaceConfigSchema`, yielding nothing when the text is not YAML or its shape is not a configuration. The compiler's configuration pass calls it on the `{Config}` source it finds, before compiling it into the manifest.

`.loom/config.yaml` is the compiled form the compiler writes and this package reads. It holds the workspace's defaults — the activated languages, the primary, the anchor, the settings, and the name of the corpus directory looms sit in. Every field is optional, because a workspace may compile from no `{Config}` source at all and still produce a manifest, an empty one.

`ResolvedConfig` is the resolved shape every reader works with: the workspace defaults that apply to one file. Its `anchor`, `primary`, `languages`, and `settings` are the same four a reader has always read. Three fields complete it. The `services` map names, for each activated language, the package that serves it — the one the language declared, or the conventional `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` when it declared none. The `packageRoot` is the absolute path the resolved file's package tangles under — the directory above the corpus folder its loom sits in — or `undefined` when the file sits in no corpus folder. The `workspaceRoot` is the absolute path of the workspace itself, the directory a book's higher-order sink resolves its directories against; it is `undefined` only when the file belongs to no workspace.

A file that resolves to nothing — no workspace, no configuration — receives the empty configuration: no anchor, no primary, no languages, no services, no root.

blocks.code:
export interface ResolvedConfig {
  readonly anchor: { readonly open?: string; readonly close?: string } | undefined
  readonly primary: string | undefined
  readonly languages: ReadonlyArray<string>
  readonly settings: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>
  readonly services: Record<string, string>
  readonly packageRoot: string | undefined
  readonly workspaceRoot: string | undefined
  readonly corpusDir: string | undefined
}

const empty: ResolvedConfig = {
  anchor: undefined,
  primary: undefined,
  languages: [],
  settings: {},
  services: {},
  packageRoot: undefined,
  workspaceRoot: undefined,
  corpusDir: undefined,
}

blocks.markdown: A workspace is the directory that holds the generated `.loom/` store. `findWorkspace` walks up from a starting directory to the first ancestor that has one, the same ancestor search a package manager runs for a project's lockfile. It stops at the filesystem root and returns nothing when no ancestor holds a store, so a path outside any Loom project resolves to the empty configuration.

`readManifest` reads the compiled `.loom/config.yaml` and decodes it. It parses the file as YAML and validates the result against `WorkspaceManifestSchema`, yielding the manifest on success. A read failure, a parse failure, or YAML the schema rejects all yield nothing — the resolver then falls back, and the editor never crashes on a broken manifest.

The `services` map is the one resolved field the manifest does not carry directly. Every activated language resolves to the package that serves it: `servicesOf` maps each language id to its service, taking the language's own `service` when it set one and the conventional `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` when it did not.

Resolving a file gives it the workspace defaults and the root its sinks tangle under. The anchor, primary, and settings are the workspace's; the languages are its activated set; the services resolve from those languages.

`containerRoot` derives the package root by convention. It walks up from the file to the nearest directory named like the corpus — `corpus` by default — and takes the directory above it, so a loom in `pkg/corpus/` tangles into `pkg/`. A file inside no corpus directory carries no root and tangles beside itself.

`corpusDir` is the other half of that walk: the corpus directory the file lives in, the one Loom enumerates to discover its sibling looms. It is the same nearest ancestor `containerRoot` finds, returned whole rather than stepped above. A file in no corpus directory — a workspace whose `corpus` is `.`, or a loose file — discovers from its own directory, never climbing out to scan a whole workspace.

`LoomConfig` is the service the rest of Loom calls. Its `resolve` takes any path inside a project — a `.loom` file, usually — and returns the configuration that applies to it: it finds the workspace, reads the compiled manifest, and resolves against it. A path with no workspace, no manifest, or no name at all resolves to the empty configuration.

Its `manifest` reads a workspace's whole compiled manifest, found by walking up from a directory — the read the activation commands and the editor's service loader share. Its `materialize` writes a manifest back to the workspace's `.loom/config.yaml`, creating the store directory if it is absent.

Effect supplies `Schema` for every shape, `Option` for a decode that may yield nothing, the `Effect` type its methods return, and `Context` and `Layer` for the service and its layer. The `yaml` package parses and serialises the compiled manifest. Bun's synchronous filesystem reads, writes, and creates directories; its path module resolves paths, walks to parent directories, and names the platform separator.
