links.name: The pieces a configuration is built from

blocks.markdown: Loom compiles a `.loom` file from its own syntax into code. The **Loom syntax** — the headings, anchors, and sinks — says how the file composes, and Loom serves it on the source itself. The **product** is the author's code, composed from the sections those anchors name. `LoomCompiler` is where the two meet: it runs the chain — `parse → product` — and projects the result for whoever asks.

`LoomCompiler` is the higher-order service that runs the chain. It composes the passes rather than re-implementing them, and hands each consumer a finished projection through a verb.

Its spine is one step. `buildCorpus` runs `parse |> product`: it loads each module through `LoomCorpusAstBuilder` — which parses the file and composes its product in the same pass — keeps it in `LoomMemo`, and discovers the rest of the corpus beside it. The corpus is the through-line every projection reads.

The product is built once, with the parse. `LoomCorpusAstBuilder` composes each module's product as it loads, so `LoomMemo` caches the whole module — parse and product together — and a second projection of an unedited file reuses it. An edit evicts the file and its dependents, so the next projection rebuilds only what changed.

The verbs project the corpus for the consumers. `compile` gives the editor its Volar tree, the source as the `loom` root and the product sections beneath it. `tangle` resolves a file's `{path}` sinks to the content and target the CLI writes. `diagnose` gives a file's Loom health, the diagnostics the editor shows. `definition` follows an anchor to the section it names, and `references` finds every anchor that names the section under the cursor. `rename` gathers the title and anchors a rename rewrites together, and `renameRange` gives the span the rename box covers, while `navigationRange` gives the whole span a definition link underlines over any navigable token. `semanticTokens` gives each of a file's tokens its colour, so the editor highlights the source. `reach` gives the files an entry pulls in, so the editor associates their scripts. `weave` projects the whole corpus into a `WovenCorpus`, the pages and navigation the website renders. `invalidate` evicts a file and the dependents that inlined it, naming what an edit must reproject.

Every verb builds the corpus the same way and differs only in what it reads. `compile` and `tangle` read the product — the composed sections and the sink files. `diagnose`, `definition`, `references`, `rename`, `renameRange`, `navigationRange`, `semanticTokens`, `reach`, and `weave` read the parse and the corpus graph, since Loom health, navigation, colour, placement, and the woven page need no product. `compile` takes its `Source` from the caller, so the editor reads the open buffer, and it guards Volar's synchronous hook: a defect degrades to a bare `loom` document carrying the file's text, and the editor keeps working rather than crashing. The other verbs read from disk through `DocumentSource`.

A file's corpus is the looms beside it: every `.loom` under the directory the file lives in, discovered from disk, not the files it names in imports. `buildCorpus` lists that directory through the source and builds each loom it finds, so a book reaches the chapters it places and an anchor reaches the section it names, with nothing imported by hand. A source that lists nothing — a single document held in memory — yields a corpus of one.

The whole corpus is what navigation resolves against, so a name reaches a section wherever in the corpus it lives. The tangle and the diagnose narrow it: each scopes to the entry and the sections it composes across files, through `reachable`, so tangling one file never reaches a sibling it should not, and a broken sibling never fails a neighbour's build.

A `Modules` map keys each module by its path. It is the value `buildCorpus` returns and every projection reads. `scopedTo` keeps the modules a set of paths names, the way the tangle narrows the whole corpus to one entry's reach.

Two verbs are about one file within its corpus, so they pick that module out once the corpus is produced. `entryOf` looks the entry up by path and dies if it is absent — a projection of a file the corpus never loaded is a bug, not a recoverable state. It returns the entry beside the corpus it came from, so a caller reads both from one result.

`tangle` is the product's file projection, the sibling of `compile`. It produces the corpus, refuses a broken build, and resolves the entry's `{Tangle}` sections to the content and target a writer emits. It scopes through `reachable` — the entry and the sections it composes — yet emits only the entry's own files, so a loom it merely transcludes never re-emits, and every file is written once.

`composition` is that same resolution turned to the editor: every root in the corpus, the code it composes and the path it composes to, ungated and unscoped. It is the live product as a filesystem — what a product language service builds its analyzer's program over, so a cross-file import resolves against a section's live composition rather than its output on disk.

`resolveSinks` gathers the files this tangle emits: the entry module's own tangle sinks — one per `[dir, file]` section — and every sink a book in the corpus routes into place. For each it flattens the composed code across the corpus with `fromProduct`, then resolves where the file lands. A module's file emits only when the entry produced it or a book routes it, so importing a module to transclude its code never re-emits that module's own files. `outputPath` places a sink under a root, so a sink names its file relative to that root rather than climbing out of the corpus. `fileRoot` chooses that root for each sink: the chapter prefix a book places the sink under, resolved against the workspace, or the package root the configuration gives when no chapter places it. A file the workspace assigns to no package falls back to the module's own directory. `resolveSinks` returns a `TangledFile` per sink: the section that produced it, where it lands, and its content. `LoomTangler` performs the writes; `tangle` itself touches no disk.

A tangle must never emit an anchor it could not resolve, nor build on a file it could not read. So `tangle` runs `corpusErrors` over the whole corpus first — every module's document and product — and fails with a `TangleError` the moment any module carries an error. A missing import, a malformed parse, an unresolved `::[…]` reference each stops the build rather than leaving a file with a silently dropped reference. The failure is loud, and names every file and line.

The navigation verbs answer in `FrameLocation`s — a path and a line-and-character range — so the editor places a result without re-reading the file. `definitionAt` and `referencesAt` give a `CorpusLocation` in the source's own offsets, since the corpus walks in offsets. `frameLocationOf` converts one to the other, reading the target module's text to turn each offset into a line and a character. `lineCharAt` counts the newlines before an offset for the line, and the distance from the last newline for the character — the zero-based pair the editor speaks. `headingLocationOf` finds a root's section by its name and gives the `FrameLocation` of its heading title, so the composition can point a cross-file `import` at the section that writes the file.

`projectTree` assembles a file's virtual-code tree. It emits the file's prose as a Markdown document with `fromProse`, one product document per composition root with `fromProduct`, and gathers them under a `loom` root with `rootVirtualCode` — the source itself, the surface Loom serves the Loom syntax on. It also hands the root the section symbols `symbolMappings` reads from the parse — each heading and anchor span — so go-to-definition and rename reach them on the file the author opened. A root is a section that no same-file anchor pulls into another, and `rootsAt` keeps those names and drops the members. `fromProduct` inlines the members a root pulls in, so each emitted document is a whole resolved Product — and a member's span maps into the root that carries it, rather than standing as a document of its own. The projection follows cross-file `::[…]` references through the corpus modules it is handed, so it treats a lone file and a whole corpus the same way.

Every model up to here is plain data, which keeps the pipeline serialisable and easy to test. Volar is not plain data: it wants a function-based `IScriptSnapshot` for a document's text and its own `CodeMapping` for each source span. `toVolar` is the one adapter that crosses from a `LoomVirtualCode` tree to Volar's `VirtualCode`, and the only place in Loom that touches Volar's runtime types. It recurses down the embedded tree, converting every node.

Three small functions do the work. `stringSnapshot` presents a string as the minimal snapshot Volar reads. `featuresOf` chooses which editor features Volar forwards at a mapped span, and for every author-facing token it defers to `LoomSymbol`: it reads the span's symbol kind through `profileOf` and forwards exactly the features that kind allows — a heading navigates, an anchor navigates and verifies, prose only folds and outlines. The table is the one place those features live, so `featuresOf` repeats none of them. It answers only for the two kinds that carry the projection's structure rather than a token a cursor rests on. A `source` span — the whole-file identity mapping the root carries — takes verification alone, so Loom's own diagnostics surface on the file while no other feature fires there. A `product` span carries the full set, verification and completion and semantics among them, so the author edits the resolved composition as if it were the source. `toCodeMapping` rewrites one of our `Mapping`s — a `.loom` source span paired with the generated span it projects to — as Volar's `CodeMapping`, choosing its features with `featuresOf`.

blocks.code:
export const stringSnapshot = (text: string): ts.IScriptSnapshot => ({
  getText: (start, end) => text.slice(start, end),
  getLength: () => text.length,
  getChangeRange: () => undefined,
})

const productFeatures: CodeMapping['data'] = {
  verification: true,
  completion: true,
  semantic: true,
  navigation: true,
  structure: true,
}

const featuresOf = (kind: Mapping['kind']): CodeMapping['data'] =>
  Match.value(kind).pipe(
    Match.when('source', () => ({ verification: true })),
    Match.when('product', () => productFeatures),
    Match.when(Match.defined, (symbol) => profileOf(symbol).features),
    Match.orElse(() => productFeatures),
  )

const toCodeMapping = (m: Mapping): CodeMapping => ({
  sourceOffsets: [m.source.start.offset],
  generatedOffsets: [m.genStart],
  lengths: [m.source.end.offset - m.source.start.offset],
  generatedLengths: [m.genLength],
  data: featuresOf(m.kind),
})

export const toVolar = (vc: LoomVirtualCode): VirtualCode => ({
  id: vc.id,
  languageId: vc.languageId,
  snapshot: stringSnapshot(vc.code),
  mappings: Array.map(vc.mappings, toCodeMapping),
  embeddedCodes: Array.map(vc.embeddedCodes, toVolar),
})

blocks.markdown:
`DocumentSource` is the compiler's disk seam. It reads a `.loom` file's text by path through Bun's synchronous `fs`, and fails with `ReadError` when the file cannot be read. The read is synchronous because the editor projects inside Volar's synchronous hook, which cannot await. The tangler injects this source; the editor injects one of its own.

It also lists a directory's looms. `list` reads the directory tree under a corpus root and returns every `.loom` path in it, the file's siblings the compiler builds into one corpus. `loomsUnder` walks the tree itself, one directory at a time, so it can prune the generated and vendored directories a corpus never includes — `node_modules`, the `.loom` store, `dist`, and `.git` — rather than descend into them and filter after. `entriesIn` reads one directory's entries and lists nothing when it cannot be read, so a path outside any project still resolves to a corpus of one.

From Volar come the `VirtualCode` and `CodeMapping` types the adapter targets. From the pipeline come its collaborators: `LoomCorpusAstBuilder` with its `Source` seam and `ReadError`; the projection functions `fromProduct`, `fromProse`, `rootNamesAt`, `rootVirtualCode`, and `symbolMappings`; the corpus model — `LoomModule`, the `corpusErrors` and `moduleDiagnostics` readers, the `reachable` and `transitiveDependents` reaches over the anchor graph, and the `definitionAt`, `referencesAt`, `renameAt`, `renameRangeAt`, and `navigationRangeAt` navigation walks with the `CorpusLocation` they yield; the virtual-code model, `LoomVirtualCode` and its `Mapping`; and the `LoomMemo` cache. The `WeaveBuilder` pass projects the whole corpus into the `WovenCorpus` the `weave` verb returns. `profileOf` and `symbolsOf` from `LoomSymbol` give each mapped span its editor features and each of a file's tokens its colour, and `normaliseTitle` folds a heading title to the name an anchor resolves. From the language-service contract come `FrameLocation`, the editor-shaped range the navigation verbs return, and `FrameToken`, a range paired with the colour `semanticTokens` returns. `Diagnostic` carries an error's message and place. Effect supplies the composition primitives, `Data` among them for the tagged `TangleError`, and Bun's `fs` and `path` read each file and resolve each sink.

blocks.markdown: `PackageConfig` reads a `.loom` file's configuration and gives the compiler the settings it needs to build that file: the anchor delimiters its sections cut with, the primary language a section without a specifier inherits, and the root its sinks tangle under. It reads through `@athrio/loom-config`, which resolves the file's configuration from the one workspace manifest — a project has no per-package configuration. This service turns that resolved configuration into the compiler's own terms.

`BuildSettings` is the answer the compiler gets for one file. It holds a full anchor delimiter pair, the file's primary language, the root the file's package tangles under, the workspace root a book's higher-order sink resolves its directories against, and the corpus directory the compiler enumerates to discover the file's sibling looms.

The anchor delimiters are always a complete pair. A `loom.json` may set one side, both, or neither, so `anchorDelimsOf` fills each missing side from `defaultAnchorDelims`, the built-in `::[` and `]`. A package overrides a delimiter only where the default would collide with its own product language.

The primary language is the language a specifier-less section inherits. It comes from the workspace configuration, and is absent when the configuration names none. The product pass reads it as the default beneath the document's own `lang` Warp, so a project can name its language once and let every document drop the Warp.

The package root is the absolute directory the file's package tangles into — the directory holding the file's `corpus/`. `@athrio/loom-config` derives it from that convention, and it is absent when the file sits in no corpus directory.

`PackageConfig` is the service the compiler depends on, so it reaches build settings through the same dependency injection it uses for every other pass. It depends on `LoomConfig` and reads through it, holding no file logic of its own. Its `resolve` takes a file's path, asks `LoomConfig` for that file's package configuration, and maps the answer to a `BuildSettings`.

Effect supplies the service and the effect type. `LoomConfig`, from `@athrio/loom-config`, finds and reads a file's `loom.json`. `AnchorDelims` and `defaultAnchorDelims` come from the token layer, where the built-in delimiters live, and `Path` types a file path.

blocks.markdown:
A fault is something the author got wrong that Loom can name: a bracket left open, a label left empty, an anchor that resolves to no section. `LoomFault` is the closed set of them, and this module is the one place that spells out each fault's wording and severity.

Writing them once is the point. A reader sees everything Loom can say, gathered in one place. A detection site — in the tokeniser, the AST builder, or the product pass — names a fault and the span it covers, and never writes the words itself. `describe` turns a fault into the message a person reads, and `faulty` wraps that message, positioned, as the `Health` a node carries. Adding a fault is adding a line to the table. The table is exhaustive, so a fault with no line does not compile.

A `LoomFault` is a tagged union, and each variant names one fault and carries only what its message needs. An `UnclosedDelimiter` carries the delimiter it expected to find. An `UnresolvedAnchor` carries the name that matched no section, and an `AmbiguousAnchor` adds the count that made it ambiguous. A `MissingWarpValue` carries the name of a Warp that bound nothing. A `CrossLanguageAnchor` carries the anchor name, the language found at the target, and the language the host section composes. An `UnresolvedTocEntry` carries the title of a table-of-contents entry that names no chapter.

The label faults fold many constructs into two variants. A construct is a named span the grammar validates — a specifier, a file path, a warp name, an anchor name. `EmptyLabel` is one with no text. `MalformedLabel` is one whose text breaks the construct's rule. Two further constructs — a warp annotation and a warp default — can only be empty, never malformed, so `EmptyConstruct` adds those two to `MalformedConstruct`.

Destructuring `taggedEnum` gives one constructor per variant, so a detection site builds a fault by name — `EmptyLabel({ construct: 'specifier' })`, or `MissingLanguageWarp()` for a variant that carries nothing.

blocks.code:
export const {
  UnclosedDelimiter,
  EmptyLabel,
  MalformedLabel,
  MissingWarpValue,
  UnresolvedAnchor,
  AmbiguousAnchor,
  CrossLanguageAnchor,
  UnresolvedTocEntry,
} = Data.taggedEnum<LoomFault>()

blocks.markdown:
`describe` reads a fault aloud. It maps each variant to a `severity` and the `message` a person sees, and it is exhaustive over the union — a new fault does not compile until it has a line here. The table cannot drift from the faults it describes.

Two small lookups carry the wording that varies by construct. `noun` gives each construct the name a reader knows it by, and `rule` gives each construct that can be malformed the rule its text broke. An empty label then reads `<noun> cannot be empty`, and a malformed one reads `<noun> <rule>; got <value>`.

A detection site holds a fault and the span it covers, and needs a `Health` for the node. `faulty` is that step: it describes the fault through the catalog and returns a health carrying the one diagnostic, positioned where the fault was found. The severity sets the node's status: an error or a warning fails it. An informational note would leave it healthy, but the catalog raises only errors and warnings — never `info`.

`Data` builds the tagged union and a constructor for each fault, and `Match` builds the exhaustive table. `@athrio/loom-ast` types what a fault becomes — the `Severity` and `Position` a diagnostic carries, and the `Health` a node holds.

blocks.markdown: `LoomTangler` is the filesystem surface of the product. The compiler resolves a `.loom` file's `{path}` sinks to their content and target; the tangler writes them to disk. `tangle` takes a path, finds the looms under it, and writes every sink each one resolves — skipping the chapters a book already places, so every file is written once. It is the half of the pipeline the editor never runs — the editor projects to virtual code, the tangler emits real files.

`LoomTangler` holds the compiler and the filesystem. `tangleFile` asks the compiler to resolve one file's sinks — which fails with a `TangleError` if any module in the corpus carries an error — then `writeFile` creates each target's directory and writes its content, returning the file it wrote. `loomsUnder` lists every `.loom` source file beneath a directory, skipping the generated stores — `node_modules`, `dist`, `.git`, and the `.loom` activation store — so it never reads one as source. `tangle` lists the looms a path covers and tangles each, so every chapter emits its own files and every file is written once.

It draws on `LoomCompiler` to resolve each file's sinks to content and target, gating a broken corpus with its `TangleError`. Effect's `FileSystem`, with Bun's path module, lists a directory's looms and writes each file. Effect supplies the array and effect primitives. `TangledFile` is the resolved sink the compiler hands back, and `Path` a file path.

blocks.markdown:
The plugin is the utility's body. It serves the overlay to the page and, through the feedback log, records the remarks the overlay captures and reads, resolves, discards, and edits them. Vite calls it; it answers through Loom's engine.

Vite drives the plugin through hooks it calls at set moments — when the configuration resolves, when the development server starts, when a page's HTML is assembled. Those hooks are a synchronous host, the way Volar is for the language server. So the plugin builds a `ManagedRuntime` once and runs each Effect on it at the hook, rather than threading Effect through Vite's own calls.

Programs answer the overlay, one per endpoint. `captureProgram` records a new remark; `feedProgram` lists them all; `resolveProgram`, `discardProgram`, and `updateProgram` each act on one entry by its sequence — marking it addressed, dropping it, or changing its words. `bodyJson` is the request-body reader they share: it gathers the body and parses it as JSON. Each program reaches the log with `yield* FeedbackLog`, so the runtime is the only thing that need supply it.

`bodyText` gathers a request's body into a string. A request arrives as a Node stream of chunks, so reading it is the one imperative corner here: the chunks collect as they land, and the promise settles when the stream ends. This is a foreign boundary, sealed off the way the log seals the filesystem.

`loomAnnotate` returns the Vite plugin. On construction it reads the overlay source beside it and transpiles the TypeScript to browser JavaScript through Bun, and it builds the runtime that provides the log over `BunServices` — the one place the platform is chosen. It applies only on `serve`, so it exists during development and vanishes from a production build.

Three hooks carry the work. `configResolved` records the resolved project root in `LOOM_ANNOTATE_LOG`, so the log lands beside the project the server is serving. `configureServer` mounts the endpoints: it matches the request's path and method and either serves the overlay, runs one of the programs, or passes the request on. `transformIndexHtml` injects the overlay's script into every page.
The plugin module is the programs it runs, the body reader they lean on, and the factory that wires them into Vite's hooks.

blocks.markdown:
The overlay writes the log; the command reads and settles it. `loom-annotate` prints every remark in a form a person — or a coding agent — takes in at a glance, and resolves or discards one by its sequence. A skill runs it, drops the list into the chat, and works the open remarks; resolving one is then a command, not a hand-edit of the log.

It finds the log itself. Given a path it uses that one; given none it searches the project for every `.loom/feedback.jsonl` — so one command covers a workspace whose apps each keep their own log.

The command reuses the log's own read, resolve, and discard operations — the same code the plugin runs — so the format and the rules for changing it live in one place. It only adds a way to find the logs, a way to print an entry, and the argument handling that chooses between them.

blocks.code:
import { Array, Console, Effect, FileSystem, Match } from 'effect'
import { BunRuntime, BunServices } from '@effect/platform-bun'
import { discardEntry, readEntries, resolveEntry } from './feedback-log'
import type { Entry } from './entry'

blocks.markdown: `logPaths` globs the project for every `.loom/feedback.jsonl`, skipping `node_modules` and passing `dot: true`, since `.loom` is a hidden directory. Walking the tree is edge work, so it sits in one `Effect.sync`.

`formatEntry` renders one remark. Every entry shows its sequence, whether it is open or resolved, its kind, and the page it was made on. An annotation adds the element it points at — the label a person reads and the selector that finds it again — then the words. The indentation keeps a remark scannable as a block.
`listProgram` reads every log it finds, counts the ones still open, and prints each log's entries under its path — the open count first, so a reader sees at once how much is left.

`changeLog` settles one remark by applying an operation to a sequence in a single log. The log is the one you name, or — with none named — the only one it finds. Without a valid sequence, or with no log or several to choose between, it prints how to call it and stops rather than guessing.

The program reads the subcommand from the first argument — `list` by default, or `resolve` or `discard` — and runs the matching one. Bun supplies the filesystem, and `BunRuntime.runMain` is the one edge it all runs from.

blocks.code:
::[What it draws on]

::[Finding the logs]

::[Formatting an entry]

::[Listing every remark]

::[Resolving and discarding]

const [command, ...rest] = process.argv.slice(2)

const program = Match.value(command ?? 'list').pipe(
  Match.withReturnType<Effect.Effect<void, unknown, FileSystem.FileSystem>>(),
  Match.when('list', () => listProgram),
  Match.when('resolve', () =>
    changeLog(
      resolveEntry,
      (seq, path) => `Resolved #${seq} in ${path}`,
      'Usage: loom-annotate resolve <seq> [log]',
      rest,
    ),
  ),
  Match.when('discard', () =>
    changeLog(
      discardEntry,
      (seq, path) => `Discarded #${seq} in ${path}`,
      'Usage: loom-annotate discard <seq> [log]',
      rest,
    ),
  ),
  Match.orElse(() =>
    Console.error('Usage: loom-annotate [list | resolve <seq> [log] | discard <seq> [log]]'),
  ),
)

program.pipe(Effect.provide(BunServices.layer), BunRuntime.runMain)

blocks.markdown: `@athrio/loom-annotate` ships as source. A project installs it as a development dependency, and its own Vite — run on Bun — reads the plugin straight from source; there is no build of its own.

The `exports` field is the package's surface — the `loomAnnotate` plugin — and the `bin` field adds the `loom-annotate` command that prints the log. The three libraries the plugin builds on are peers: `effect` and `@effect/platform-bun` for the runtime and the log, and `vite` for the plugin type. A project using the plugin already has all three, so it supplies them. The same three sit in `devDependencies` so the package type-checks on its own.

The config extends the workspace base and typechecks `src`. It widens the library beyond the base's, because the overlay runs in a browser and reaches for the document, `fetch`, and the DOM: the `DOM` and `DOM.Iterable` libraries type those. The config never ships.

The overlay is the surface you point with. It is a small widget the plugin injects into the page during development: a launcher in the corner, a chat panel that lists the remarks so far, and a pick mode that highlights whatever element the cursor is over.

Two flows lead into the chat. Type a message in the panel and press return, and it lands as a remark. Or start pick mode — which hides the chat so the page is clear — hover to the element you mean, and click it. A small note appears where you can write what you saw; return, or the add button, files it against that element. Escape leaves pick mode. Hiding the chat only tucks it away; the panel and its history stay. Each remark in the chat carries its own controls: edit its words, resolve it, or discard it.

The overlay is the utility's one imperative corner, the way CodeMirror is a reader's. It owns a piece of the page and mutates it as you point and type, which a pure view cannot do. So it stays sealed: it lives inside a shadow root, keeps its own handful of state, and speaks to the plugin over three plain fetches. Everything behind those fetches — the log, the entry, the runtime — is the Effect program of the earlier chapters. Bun transpiles this file to browser JavaScript, and the plugin serves it; it imports nothing.

The overlay speaks to the plugin in JSON over the endpoints the plugin mounts. `post` sends a remark and `getFeed` lists them all. An entry, as the overlay sees it, is a sequence number, a kind, the words written, an optional element label, and whether it has been addressed.

`capture` records where an annotation points. It reads three things from the element: a CSS selector that finds it again, a label that names it in conversation — its tag and a little of its text — and the rectangle it occupies now. The selector is a best effort: an element with an id needs only that; otherwise it walks up to five ancestors, marking each by its tag and, where siblings share it, its position. This ancestor walk is the sealed widget's one loop.

`entryLine` renders one entry as a row in the chat: a glyph for its kind, the words, and — for an annotation — the element's label, with a set of controls that appear on hover to edit, resolve, or discard it. The rows keep the order they were made in, so no sequence number is shown, though each control still carries the sequence to name its entry. A resolved entry dims but stays, and loses its resolve control. When an entry is being edited, its row becomes a text field and a save. `controlsRow` is the button set, and one listener on the list serves them all. The written text is escaped, since it is shown as markup.

blocks.markdown: The overlay wears the editor palette so it reads as part of the tool: a dark panel, a mint accent, a monospace face. The primary buttons — send and add — carry the accent and dim when the field they answer to is empty. The note popover rises from the bottom, bordered in the accent, over the element you just picked.

`start` builds the widget and wires it. It holds three pieces of state: whether pick mode is on, the element a note is pending against, and which entry is being edited. `wire` is the shared rule for a field and its button — the button disables while the field is empty, return fires it, and shift-return still breaks a line. `act` posts a change to one entry and refreshes. One delegated listener on the chat list serves every entry's controls — edit turns the row into a field, resolve and discard settle it, save and cancel end an edit — and a second lets return save an edit in place. Pick mode listens on the page in the capture phase: it hides the chat, highlights the element under the cursor, and claims the next click. A claimed click captures the element and opens the note; filing it posts the annotation and brings the chat back. Escape backs out — of the note, or of pick mode — and returns the chat. The panel drags wider from its left edge, the fields grow with what you type up to a limit, and the message draft is saved as you write, so an unexpected reload does not lose it.

The overlay module is how it talks to the plugin, how it names an element, how it renders the log, the look it wears, and the widget that ties those together. It mounts once, when the page has a body to mount into.

blocks.markdown:
The annotation utility is a Vite plugin for reviewing a running app by pointing at it. While the development server runs, the plugin draws an overlay over the page. You click an element to leave a remark on it, or type a message into a sidebar. Each remark is saved to a log that a coding agent reads.

A review is a conversation about what you see, and the utility keeps that conversation in one place and in order. Remarks do not fly off to a chat one at a time. They accumulate — an element here, a sentence there — in the order you make them. When you are ready, the agent reads the whole log at once and treats it as a single prompt, deciding what to act on now and what to leave for later.

Nothing about this lives in the app itself. The plugin is a development dependency, and it runs only while the development server runs. A production build carries no trace of it. The same plugin annotates any Vite app, so it stays a small tool beside the code rather than a feature inside it.

The utility has four parts, one per chapter. This chapter defines the entry, the unit the log is made of. The feedback log stores those entries on disk. The Vite plugin serves the overlay and receives what it captures. The overlay is the surface you point with.

The log is a list of entries, and an entry is one remark. A remark is one of two kinds. An annotation points at an element on the page. A message stands on its own, the way a sentence typed into a chat does. The two share a page — the route they were made on — and the words you wrote.

An annotation also records where it points. It carries a CSS selector that finds the element again, a short label that names it the way you would in conversation — its tag and a little of its text — and the rectangle it occupied on screen when you clicked.

blocks.markdown:
The overlay sends a draft: a remark with its words and, for an annotation, its anchor. The draft says nothing about where it sits in the log. The log decides that, stamping each draft with a sequence number that fixes its order, the time it arrived, and an `addressed` flag that starts false and turns true once the agent has dealt with it.

So the two schemas differ only by that stamp. A `DraftSchema` is what the overlay posts; an `EntrySchema` is what the log stores. Keeping them apart lets the plugin parse an incoming draft at its edge and trust every field inward, while the sequence and the time stay the log's to assign.

`stampDraft` turns a draft into an entry by adding the three fields the log owns. It matches on the draft's kind so each branch builds the entry that belongs to it, and `Match.exhaustive` makes a new kind of remark a compile error until this function handles it too.
The entry module is the rectangle a remark points to, the fields the two kinds of remark share, the draft and the entry, and the stamp that turns one into the other.

blocks.markdown: The feedback log is where remarks accumulate. It is a file of entries, one per line, in the order they were made — a format that reads plainly, appends cleanly, and commits to git without a merge fight. The log offers five operations: read every entry, record a new one, mark one addressed, discard one, and edit one's words.

The log lives at `.loom/feedback.jsonl` under the project root. The Vite plugin knows the resolved root and passes it down through the `LOOM_ANNOTATE_LOG` environment variable; when that variable is absent — a test, say — the log falls back to the same path under the current directory.

blocks.code:
import { Option } from 'effect'
import { dirname, resolve } from 'node:path'

const logPath = (): string =>
  Option.match(Option.fromNullishOr(process.env.LOOM_ANNOTATE_LOG), {
    onSome: (path) => path,
    onNone: () => resolve(process.cwd(), '.loom', 'feedback.jsonl'),
  })

blocks.markdown: `readEntries` reads the file and decodes each line into an entry. A missing file is not a failure — an untouched project simply has no log yet — so an absent file reads as no entries. Each line is parsed from JSON and decoded against `EntrySchema`, which rejects a line that does not match either kind of remark.

`writeEntries` replaces the file with a fresh rendering of every entry. The log is small — a review is dozens of remarks, not millions — so recording and resolving both rewrite the whole file rather than seek within it. Writing first ensures the `.loom` directory exists, then joins the entries into lines.

`recordDraft` appends one remark. It reads what is there, stamps the draft with the next sequence number and the current time, writes the log back with the new entry at its end, and returns that entry. The sequence number is one past the highest in the log, so a number is never reused — even after a remark in the middle is discarded, later ones keep their sequences. The time comes from Effect's clock rather than a bare `Date`, so a test can fix it.

`resolveEntry` marks one remark addressed. The agent calls it once it has dealt with an entry, so the next read shows only what is still open. It reads every entry, turns the `addressed` flag true on the one whose sequence matches, and writes them all back. A resolved remark stays in the log as a record; it is not removed.

`discardEntry` drops a remark for good — the one whose sequence matches, gone from the log. `editEntry` changes a remark's words in place and leaves all else about it untouched. Both, like resolving, read the whole log, change the one entry, and write it back.

`FeedbackLog` is the service the plugin holds. Its `make` reads the filesystem once and resolves the log path once, then binds every operation to that filesystem and path. The service leaves the filesystem out of its own layer: the plugin provides `BunServices` at the runtime edge, the one place the platform is chosen.
The log module is where it lives, how it reads and writes, the operations that change it, and the service that offers them all.

blocks.markdown: `LoomAstBuilder` is the final parse stage: `build` turns a stream of wefts into a `LoomDocument`. A weft before the first heading stands at the document's top level, as frontmatter or preamble; a heading gathers the body wefts up to the next heading into a section. `build` groups the wefts along that line, then folds each group into the document. The module also holds `emptyDocument`, the empty error-carrying tree a caller falls back to when it cannot produce a real one.

Grouping a heading with its body means looking ahead to the next heading, so `build` gathers the whole weft stream first with `Stream.runCollect`. `groupNodes` then splits the wefts into the document's top-level nodes, `appendToDocument` folds each node into a growing document, and `makeDocument` finishes it.

`groupNodes` splits the flat weft list at the first heading. What comes before stands at the document's top level — its frontmatter and preamble — so `isTopLevelWeft` keeps those two kinds. From the first heading on, `Array.chop` walks the rest heading by heading: each `sectionChunk` takes a heading and the body wefts up to the next heading, and `buildSection` turns that pair into a `LoomSection`. Every chunk leads with a heading, so `sectionChunk` guards on `isHeading`; a well-formed file never trips that guard. The `isHeading`, `isSectionBody`, `isPreamble`, and `isToc` predicates classify each weft.

The fold routes each peeled node. `appendToDocument` appends a `LoomSection` to the document's sections, a `PreambleWeft` to its preamble, and a `FrontmatterWeft` to its frontmatter, starting from the empty `initialDocument`.

`buildSection` turns a heading and its body chunk into a `LoomSection`: it partitions the body into preamble, code, and toc-entry wefts, then derives the section's source and span. Only a `{TOC}` section carries entries; every other section leaves that run empty. `headingOf` lifts the heading weft to a `LoomHeading`; `sourceOf` concatenates the children's sources to reconstruct the byte range; `spanFrom` runs the span from the heading's start to the last child's end.

`makeFrontmatter` gathers a file's frontmatter wefts into one `LoomFrontmatter`, or nothing when the file opened with no block. The membership fields come from two lines: the part and its name from one, the chapter and its title from the other.

`frontmatterField` reads a `key: value` field's value by name — the `Package` a `{Tangle}` section writes to, or the `Language` its sections inherit — lifting the missing case to `Option` at the `.find`.

`makeDocument` assembles the final `LoomDocument` from the folded frontmatter, preamble, and sections, building the frontmatter block through `makeFrontmatter`. `documentSpan` spans the whole, from the first node to the last. A document needs no `lang` Warp: a section without one inherits the workspace's primary language, so an absent declaration is no fault.

Some failures leave the chain with no tree to fold. `emptyDocument` is the recovery: an empty document — no preamble, no sections — whose root health carries one error diagnostic at the document's span. A caller that cannot produce a real document returns this one. So a `.loom` always parses to a tree, and the error rides in that tree's own health rather than being thrown.

`emptyDocumentFor` is the mixed-terminator case. The parse chain cannot pin a mixed line terminator to any node, so it raises `MixedEOL`. `emptyDocumentFor` names the two conventions and the lines they sit on, then builds the empty document from that message. `eolName` gives each convention — `LF`, `CRLF`, or `CR` — a name a person reads.

Effect supplies `Stream` to gather the weft stream, `Array` to group and fold it, and `Match`, `Option`, `Context`, and `Layer` besides. The container schemas come from `LoomAst` and the frontmatter value token from `LoomTokens`; `Position` and `okHealth` from the foundation; the `MixedEOL` type from the line scanner; and the weft types from `Weft`.

blocks.markdown: `LineRanges` is the parser's first pass. `LoomSourceRanges` turns a file's text into a stream of half-open `[start, end)` ranges, one per line — the offsets every later pass slices against and Volar maps from. It detects the file's line-terminator convention once and refuses a file that mixes conventions, the one failure the parse chain cannot pin to a node.

A file ends its lines one of three ways, named by `EolKind`: a line feed (`lf`), a carriage-return and line-feed pair (`crlf`), or a bare carriage return (`cr`). An `Eol` bundles a detected kind with two regexes — `pattern` to find its own terminator, `stray` to spot any other. `MixedEOL` is the error raised when a file uses more than one; it carries human-friendly line numbers, since mixed terminators are invisible on screen.

`detectEol` scans for the first terminator and adopts it as the file's convention, returning nothing for a single line that has none. `eolOf` builds the `Eol` for a kind — the `pattern` that walks its lines and the `stray` that catches a foreign terminator.

`checkMixed` confirms a file holds to its convention throughout: it succeeds with the `Eol` when no stray terminator appears, and fails with `MixedEOL` otherwise. `lineOfOffset` counts visual lines up to an offset, treating every terminator kind as one break, so it reports the right line even when terminators are mixed. `strayKind` names the offending terminator from its matched text.

A `LineRange` is a half-open `[start, end)` pair of byte offsets, terminator included; `LineRangeSchema` mirrors it for schema fields, both offsets non-negative integers. `lineRanges` streams them by walking the text with the convention's `pattern` — each step emits the range up to the end of the next terminator, and the last line, having none, runs to the end of the text. The source string is never split or copied; later passes slice it on demand.

`LoomSourceRanges` is the pass as an Effect service. Its `stream` detects the convention, checks for a mix — failing with `MixedEOL` — then streams the ranges. A single line with no terminator yields one range covering the whole text.
Effect supplies `Stream` for the ranges, `Data` for the tagged `MixedEOL` error, `Schema` for `LineRangeSchema`, and `Option` and `Effect` for the rest.

blocks.markdown: `WeftTokeniser` is the tokeniser stage: it fills each weft's subtokens by kind. A heading gets its title, its language specifier, and its sink. Arrow and code wefts get their `::[name]` anchors; tilde, preamble, and prose wefts get their inline content and their prose anchors, read only where Markdown leaves them live. A frontmatter field gets its part, chapter, and title, or its key and value. After this stage no weft remains `incomplete`.

Prose carries anchors too, so the tokeniser respects Markdown: an anchor inside an inline code span or a fenced block stays inert, the literal text it appears to be. An inline span is a per-line affair the prose handlers mask, but a fence spans many lines, so `tokeniseWefts` threads its state through the weft stream with `Stream.mapAccum`. `fenceStep` advances that state: a prose line of three backticks opens or closes a fence, a code chunk or a heading resets it, and it reports `skipAnchors` on every line a fence covers. `tokeniseWeft` then dispatches each weft by kind to the per-weft handlers, telling each whether to skip its anchors.

A frontmatter field is one `key: value` line, and `tokeniseFrontmatter` settles it by its key. It splits on the first colon and classifies what it finds. A `Part <Roman>` line announces a part, a `Chapter <n>` line a chapter — each carrying an ordinal past its keyword, and a name beside it. The joined `Part <Roman>, Chapter <n>` form carries a chapter by the part it belongs to, split at the comma. `Package` and `Language` are plain keys. A fence weft passes through whole, and a line that is no field settles bare, with no subtokens.

Three builders shape the tokens. `ordinalToken` reads a `Part III` or `Chapter 1` marker, its value the ordinal past the keyword. `fieldToken` builds a plain-valued token — a name, a title, a key, or a path. `bareFrontmatter` is the fence's passthrough and the fallback for a line that is no field: a weft with no subtokens.

`tokeniseToc` settles one line of a contents section, trying two shapes in turn. A `####` heading is a part: its text past the marker becomes a `TocPart`. A numbered item like `5. The multi-file AST` is a chapter: the number becomes a `TocChapter` and the text a `TocTitle`, so a later pass can resolve the entry to the chapter that number and title name. Any other line — a blank spacer — settles bare, with no subtokens, the way a frontmatter line that is no field does.

These handlers fill the body wefts. `inlineAfter` pulls a line's inline content after its marker — the code after `=>`, the prose after `~`. `tokeniseArrow` and `tokeniseCode` scan a code line for its `::[name]` anchors. `tokeniseTilde`, `tokenisePreamble`, and `tokeniseProse` scan a prose line for its anchors, and the preamble for its warps too. `maskInlineCode` blanks an inline code span before the scan, and `blankIfSkipped` blanks the whole line inside a fence, so a token survives only where Markdown leaves it live. Each aggregates its subtokens' health into the weft.

`aggregateStatus` folds a list of statuses up the lattice ok < incomplete < warning < error, the worst winning. `span` builds a position; `contentEnd` is the offset where a line's content ends, before its newline terminator, and is the bound every construct slices and positions against, so the EOL never leaks into a label, an anchor, or a diagnostic; `missingClosing` is the error health for an unclosed bracket, its diagnostic spanning the open delimiter to that content end so the squiggle lands on the construct rather than the gap after it. `brokenLabel` is the error health for a label the schema rejected. It raises an `EmptyLabel` or a `MalformedLabel` fault through the catalog, keyed by construct, so the same wording reaches the editor wherever a label is built.

A label that fails its strict pattern must still be kept. `buildSpecifierLabel` and `buildSinkFileLabel` each try the schema, and on rejection build a valid token with an empty value, the error health `brokenLabel` renders, and the rejected text stashed in `unexpected` — strict validation stays on, and bytes are never dropped. The sink builder reuses the `path` construct for its broken health, since a malformed file name is a malformed path.

`makeScanner` reads a token schema's `Probe` once and returns a per-line scanner that emits a typed token at every match, with real positions and ok health. The scanners for the specifier and sink delimiters are built from it; the schema's literal `value` guards against probe drift.

Each scanner finds opens and closes independently; construction pairs them. `partitionFirstClose` matches an open with the first close after it. `constructSpecifier` pairs `{` and `}` into a language label. `constructSink` pairs `[` and `]` into a sink, its inner text the file a `{Tangle}` section emits. When no close follows, each synthesises one at the line's content end — the offset before the newline — so a missing bracket reports once, as `expected closing`, and the captured label never swallows the line's terminator.

Warps and anchors no longer share delimiters: a warp is `{{…}}` in a preamble line, an anchor is `::[…]` in a code line. `pairWarpDelims` pairs a preamble line's `{{` and `}}` greedily left to right — pairing an unmatched open with a synthetic `}}` at end-of-line, and surfacing unmatched closes as stray `unexpected` tokens. Anchors pair differently: `scanAnchorOpen` finds the `::[` opens, and each closes on the first `]` after it, found directly rather than scanned, because `]` is ordinary in product code — only the one closing an open is structural, and a bare `]` is never a stray.

A warp's content is `name [: type] = value`. `sliceTop` cuts at the first top-level `,` or `;`, tracking bracket depth, since a warp declares one binding; `trimSpan` trims whitespace and reports the trimmed span. `buildWarpName` mirrors the label builders. `buildOpaqueSegment` produces the opaque annotation and default tokens, flagging an empty value and returning any trailing content as `unexpected`.

`buildWarp` reads a warp's content as `name [: type] = value`. It splits on the binding `=` first — the value is what a Warp binds — then splits the part before it on `:` to separate the name from an optional type. `findAssign` finds that binding `=`: the first one that is a plain assignment, never part of `=>`, `==`, `>=`, `<=`, or `!=`, so a value that holds those operators is read whole. A Warp with no value binds nothing, so `buildWarp` faults it with `MissingWarpValue`. `splitDecl` separates the name from its optional type, and `assembleWarp` gathers the parts, aggregates their health, and carries the missing-value fault and any extras.

`buildWarpAnchor` reads a `::[…]` reference's content as its name — an identifier or a multi-word heading name. Content the anchor pattern rejects (empty) yields an error-health name and surfaces the text as `unexpected`. `anchorTargetAt` reads an optional `(path)` right after the close — the `.loom` file a directory anchor places. The path is opaque here, for a later pass to resolve; an open paren with no close stays product code. `anchorSpecifierAt` reads a `{…}` or `[…]` in that same spot instead — a misplaced language or sink — through `constructSpecifier` or `constructSink`, so it reads the same as a heading's; an open with no close stays product code, never a broken specifier. A target and a specifier exclude each other: the one character after the close picks at most one. `assembleAnchor` gathers the parts and stretches the anchor's span across whichever trails it.

blocks.title: Scanning a line for warps and anchors

blocks.markdown: `constructWarps` scans a line, pairs the `{{`/`}}` delimiters, and builds a warp per pair. `constructAnchors` scans a line for `::[` opens and builds an anchor for each, closing it on the first `]` that follows. Both take a scan text apart from the line itself, so a prose handler passes the line with its Markdown code spans blanked — a token inside backticks is found nowhere, while the one outside still reads its content from the original line. There is no fallback: a token is always a token. A malformed anchor — unclosed, or empty-named — is kept as an anchor carrying error health, never reinterpreted as literal code; an anchor whose name does not resolve likewise stays an anchor. Each is a diagnostic Loom reports, and a hard failure at tangle. The rare literal `::[` in product code is written through the host language's character escape, so the tokeniser never has to guess whether a `::[` was meant.
`headingTitle` is the text between the heading marker and the first structural token, trimmed. It returns nothing when that span is empty, and text after the first structural token is dropped.
`scanHeading` builds a heading's subnodes — the language specifier, the sink, and the title — from one line, recording the spans they consume so the title knows where to stop.

`tokeniseHeading` assembles the heading weft from its scanned subnodes — the title, the specifier, and the sink — and aggregates their health. The title names the section: `normaliseTitle` turns it into an identifier — `What the compiler draws on` becomes `WhatTheCompilerDrawsOn` — which the product pass reads to identify the section. Naming makes no name unique; two titles that normalise alike are a duplicate the diagnostics mark.

Effect supplies `Stream` and `Match` for the per-weft pass, `Schema` for decoding, and the rest. The token schemas and `getProbe` come from `LoomTokens`, the weft schemas from `Weft`, and the health and `UnexpectedToken` types from the foundation. `LoomFault` gives `faulty` and the fault constructors each diagnostic here is built from.

blocks.markdown:
`LoomCorpusAstBuilder` compiles one `.loom` file. It runs the file through every pass in turn — read its bytes, parse, build the product — and returns a `LoomModule`: the parsed document beside its `product`, the author's code with each anchor resolved to an edge. The product is built here, straight from the parse; nothing stands between the document and its product.

It builds that one module and no more. It discovers no corpus and keeps no cache — `LoomCompiler` does both, listing the file's sibling looms and memoising each result in `LoomMemo`. A cross-file `::[…]` reference stays unresolved here as well. It is inlined later, at projection, by `fromProduct`.

The builder owns no files. It reads through a `Source` the caller hands it, so the same build runs over a real filesystem for the tangler, a Volar document host for the editor, or an in-memory fake for a test.

`Source` is what the builder reads through — a small interface the caller provides, never the filesystem directly. Its `read` method returns a file's text by its path, and fails with a `ReadError` when the file cannot be read. `LoomCompiler` supplies the real implementation, `DocumentSource`, so the builder reads through the caller rather than the filesystem.

`list` is the other half: it returns every `.loom` path under a directory, the looms `LoomCompiler` discovers as a file's corpus. It is an `Option`: a caller that hands in a single document in memory carries `Option.none`, and the compiler then sees a corpus of one. The disk source the CLI and the editor use carries `Option.some` of the real listing.

`ReadError` names a file the source cannot read — one that is missing, or one the operating system refuses to open — and carries the path and the underlying cause. Its message reads `Cannot read <path>: <cause>`. The build catches it and recovers, so a file that cannot be read becomes a diagnostic on that file rather than a crash.

`build` runs one file through the whole chain, each pass consuming the one before it. `parsed` reads the file and parses it: `LoomSourceRanges` cuts the text into line ranges, `WeftClassifier` then `WeftTokeniser` turn those into tokenised wefts, and `LoomAstBuilder` folds the wefts into a `LoomDocument`. `build` then runs `ProductBuilder` over the document to compose its `product`, and stops there. The passes are peers here — no pass hides another behind a single call. Two settings resolved for the file ride in from the caller and thread through the chain: the anchor delimiters `WeftTokeniser` cuts with, and the primary language `ProductBuilder` falls back to.

The chain is total: it never throws, and it always yields a module. Three failures can stop it before a real document exists. A file the source cannot read raises `ReadError`; a mixed line terminator the parse chain cannot pin to a node raises `MixedEOL`; and a delimiter pair the package configured badly raises one of the self-describing `InvalidAnchorDelims` errors. `parsed` catches all three and recovers to an empty document carrying the error — `emptyDocument` with the read failure's message, `emptyDocumentFor` for a mixed terminator, and `emptyDocument` with the delimiter error's own `message`. So `build` runs the product pass over that document like any other. A file that cannot be read still yields a module, one whose health carries the read error. Every other malformation rides in the document's own health.

The builder draws the four parse passes — `LoomSourceRanges`, `WeftClassifier`, `WeftTokeniser`, and `LoomAstBuilder`, with `emptyDocument` and `emptyDocumentFor` for its recoveries — then `ProductBuilder` for the product pass beyond parsing. From the token layer it takes `checkAnchorDelims` and the `InvalidAnchorDelims` errors it raises, which guard the configured delimiters before the parse, alongside `AnchorDelims` and `defaultAnchorDelims`. The `LoomDocument`, `LoomModule`, and `Path` types name what `parsed` returns, what a build returns, and how a file is addressed. Effect supplies the composition primitives, `Data` for the tagged `ReadError` among them.

blocks.markdown: `WeftClassifier` labels each line with its weft kind by mode. It is a Mealy machine: the state is the previous weft and whether a heading has been seen, the input is a pattern probe of the line, and the output weft also feeds the next state. The wefts it emits are partial — the leading marker filled, the weft `incomplete` — and the tokeniser settles the rest.

The state opens before the first heading — the Document Preamble — and tracks whether a heading has been emitted, whether the line sits inside a frontmatter block, whether it sits inside a table-of-contents section, and whether it sits inside a fenced block. `classifyWefts` threads that state through the line-range stream with `Stream.mapAccum`, classifying each line and carrying the result forward. A `---` toggles the frontmatter flag: the first one opens the block, the next closes it. A `{TOC}` heading opens a contents section, and the next top-level `#` heading closes it — so the toc flag follows every heading, set when the heading bears `{TOC}` and cleared by any other. A code fence — a line of three backticks — toggles the fence flag the same way, but only in prose. Inside a fenced block a person quotes a loom verbatim, so a `#`, `=>`, or `~` there reads as text, not as a mark. A note is a third such region: `:::[Note]` opens it and a lone `:::` closes it, and the Markdown body stays inert between them, so a reader's note never reads as loom structure. A note and a fence never open inside each other.

`probeWeft` is the decision table. A `---` on the first line opens a frontmatter block, and every line through the closing `---` is a frontmatter weft; outside that block the rest of the table takes over. A code fence opens a fenced block the same way. Every line inside it is verbatim until the fence closes, so a `#`, `=>`, or `~` there stays text — the block quotes a loom, it does not compose one. A note region — `:::[Note]` to `:::` — is inert in just the same way, so a reader's Markdown body never composes. A fenced line is a preamble weft in the document preamble, where nothing else may appear, and a prose weft inside a section. Inside a table-of-contents section every line is a toc weft — a deeper `##`–`######` heading and a `1.` list item alike — so a part heading never opens a section and a chapter entry never reads as prose; only the next top-level `#` closes the section and reads as an ordinary heading again. A `#` line is a heading in any other mode, and opens a section in preamble mode. Before the first heading every line is a preamble weft, since no transition fires there. After it, the previous weft's mode decides. In preamble, `=>` opens code and `~` opens prose. In code, `~` returns to prose and every other line stays code. In prose, `=>` re-enters code and every other line stays prose. A line of dashes elsewhere is ordinary content, classified by the current mode like any other line — there is no separator weft.
`linePos` is a whole-line span; `span` is an arbitrary offset range on a line. Both stamp the line number the classifier tracks.

Two axes drive the table. `modeOf` derives the mode — preamble, code, or prose — from the previous weft's type; a frontmatter weft and a toc weft both read as preamble, since neither sits in a section body. `probeOf` recognises the line as a heading, arrow, or tilde marker, or plain text, using the same `Probe` regexes the token schemas carry. `isFrontmatterFence` tests one more marker: the `---` that opens or closes the frontmatter block. `headingLevel` counts a heading's `#` marks, so the top-level heading that closes a contents section is told from a deeper one, and `isTocHeading` tests a heading line for a `{TOC}` specifier, the marker that opens that section. `isCodeFence` tests a line for the three backticks that bound a fenced block, and `nextFence` toggles the fence flag from it — opening only on a prose weft, which `isProseWeft` identifies, so a fence inside a code region is left to that region's own language. `isNoteOpen` and `isNoteClose` test the `:::[Note]` and `:::` markers that bound a note, and `nextNote` toggles its flag the same way; each takes the other's flag and refuses to open while it is set, so a note and a fence never nest.

A constructor emits the partial weft for each kind, carrying `incompleteHealth` for the tokeniser to settle. `makeHeadingWeft` fills the `headingStart` marker, and `makeArrowWeft` and `makeTildeWeft` fill the `=>` and `~` markers from the probe match.
A plain line bears no marker, so its constructor takes none. `makePreambleWeft`, `makeCodeWeft`, and `makeProseWeft` build the ordinary lines of the three modes.

A frontmatter line splits two ways. `makeFrontmatterFence` builds a `---`, already whole, and `makeFrontmatterField` a membership or `key: value` line, left for the tokeniser to fill. `makeTocWeft` builds one line of a contents section the same way — a part heading, a chapter item, or a blank — its parts left for the tokeniser.

Effect supplies `Stream` for the accumulating scan and `Match` for the dispatch. The `LineRange` type comes from the line scanner, the marker probes and schemas from `LoomTokens`, the weft schemas from `Weft`, and `incompleteHealth`, `okHealth`, and `Position` from the foundation.

blocks.markdown: `LoomVirtualCodeBuilder` produces the `LoomVirtualCode` a `.loom` presents to Volar, in two projections that share one model. `fromProduct` walks a section's `Code` and emits its de re product, inlining transclusions by following `Ref`s across the corpus. `fromProse` blanks a file's code and emits what remains as Markdown. `fromProduct` folds its pieces with a monoid, and `rootVirtualCode` assembles a file's tree — the source as the de dicto root, the product sections and prose beneath it. Pure and total.

`fromProduct` resolves a `Ref`'s `{ path, name }` target against the corpus modules. `lookup` finds the module at the path, takes its de re `product`, and finds the `Code` whose `origin` carries the name. A module the run has not produced yet, or one with no section by that name, resolves to nothing.

A childless `LoomVirtualCode` is the unit a walk produces and folds. `concat` appends one after another, shifting the later one's mapping offsets past the earlier's code; the first's identity wins. `leaf` makes a unit, and `emptyLeaf` is the seed.

The de re walk inlines a section's fragments. `inlineComposed` folds them left to right, carrying both the text emitted so far and each fragment's successor, so it can tell whether an anchor stands alone on its line; a `Ref` that does is re-indented to that line's indent (the next section), and every other fragment — an anchor with code beside it among them — is appended as it stands. The fold also carries a flag: after an anchor that owned its line, the fragment that follows opens with the spaces the author left past the anchor, so the fold trims them. `inlinePart` handles each one: a `Fragment` becomes a `product` leaf mapped to its origin; a reference is followed and inlined, contributing nothing when its target is unresolved, missing, or cyclic. Every reference is a same-document `NameRef` sharing the consuming scope, so a transcluded block maps to the named section's own source. `absorbTrailingNewline`, with `clipMappings`, sheds a transcluded block's trailing newline so the sink's layout becomes the output's.

An anchor that stands alone on its line carries that line's indentation into what it pulls in. Write `::[body]` four spaces deep and every line of the inlined block lands four spaces deep, not just the first. For Python or Scala 3, where indentation marks block structure, this is what lets a transcluded block land at the depth its anchor sits at rather than break the file.

An anchor owns its line when only spaces and tabs sit between the line break and the anchor, and only whitespace between the anchor and the next break. `aloneBefore` and `aloneAfter` test the two sides. The trailing whitespace `aloneAfter` allows is the spacebar an author taps out of habit after the anchor: it never disqualifies the anchor, and `trimLeadingBlank` strikes it from the next fragment so the block's last line carries no stray spaces. An anchor with real code beside it — `result = ::[body]`, or `::[body] more()` — stays inline, its block pulled in as written with no re-indentation.

When the anchor owns its line, the whitespace opening that line is the indent. `lineIndentOf` reads it off the text folded so far, where the current line is whitespace to the anchor, and it is taken verbatim — the spaces and tabs the author wrote, exactly.

`indentBlock` prepends that indent to every line of the block but the first, which already sits after the indent its own fragment wrote. A blank line stays blank — it takes no indent — so the output grows no trailing whitespace.

The inserted indent is synthetic. It has no origin in any `.loom`, so no mapping may claim it, or the editor would map a click on the indent into the source. `reindentMapping` cuts each mapping at the block's line starts, so the indent always falls in the gap between two pieces and never inside one. A piece then shifts by the indentation added on the lines above it, and re-derives its source per line through `advance`, walking the block text to the cut. Every mapping tracks its source one-to-one — each is a `Fragment`'s span — so the same derivation serves them all.

`lineStartsOf` gives each line's offset in the original block, and `newStartsOf` the offset of its content once the indents are in. `lineIndexOf` finds which line a cut falls on.

Loom's navigation and colour serve the root mirror alone — the document whose map to the source is the identity. The root carries one whole-file span of the `source` kind, which Volar forwards for verification only, so a cursor on a token reaches no other Loom feature through it. `symbolMappings` adds the spans that route the rest: one span per symbol the document declares, each carrying its kind. `LoomCompiler` reads that kind's profile from `LoomSymbol` and forwards exactly the features it allows, so go-to on a heading, rename of a warp, and colour on an arrow each reach the right token through the root.

`symbolsOf` classifies every token in one pass — the heading titles and anchors, the warps, the specifiers and sinks, the arrows and tildes. `spanOf` makes one identity mapping over a token's span, since the root's code is the source verbatim.

blocks.code:
const spanOf = (position: Position, kind: MappingKind): Mapping => ({
  genStart: position.start.offset,
  genLength: position.end.offset - position.start.offset,
  source: position,
  kind,
})

export const symbolMappings = (doc: LoomDocument): ReadonlyArray<Mapping> =>
  Array.map(symbolsOf(doc), (symbol) => spanOf(symbol.position, symbol.kind))

blocks.markdown:
`fromProduct` is the de re pass — a section's product in its own language. Its virtual-code id is the section name lowercased, since Volar requires every embedded-code id to be lowercase. A section absent from the corpus yields an empty document. `fromProse` is the prose pass — the file as a Markdown document, its `languageId` `prose`, every code chunk blanked to spaces so only the prose shows while its offsets stay put. `proseMappings` maps each prose token to its own source span — the document and section preambles, every heading, and the prose between code blocks — so a mapping is precise per token, never a whole line or the whole file. `rootVirtualCode` assembles a file's tree: the source itself as the `loom` root, the prose and product documents its children. The root carries a whole-file identity span of the `source` kind, so Loom's own diagnostics reach the editor on the file the author opened, and the section-symbol spans beside it, so navigation and rename reach each heading and anchor there.

`rootNamesAt` names a file's composition roots — the sections no reference folds in. It reads the file's `product.code` from the corpus, the sections the run produced. Every reference is a same-file `NameRef`, a name anchor naming a section in this file, and it demotes its target: the target becomes a fragment of the referring section's root, sharing that scope, not a unit of its own. The sections left over, named by no reference, are the roots. The editor type-checks them and lets each fold in the fragments it names. The names come back lowercased, to match the de re ids `fromProduct` assigns.
`LoomVirtualCodeBuilder` wraps `fromProduct` as an Effect service for callers already inside Effect — the corpus compiler; the single-file editor path uses the functions directly.

Effect supplies `Array`, `Option`, and `pipe` for the folds and the resolution, and `Effect` itself for the service. The de re node types and `keyOf` come from `ProductAst`, `Position` from the foundation, and the `LoomModule` and `Path` the resolution walks from the corpus model. The parse `LoomDocument` comes from the AST, and `symbolsOf` from `LoomSymbol` classifies its tokens for the symbol mappings. The virtual-code types come from `LoomVirtualCode`.

blocks.markdown:
`LoomLanguage` gives the **Frame** its editor support — the de dicto composition
program Loom builds — the `Context.Service` classes and the Warp wiring. Loom is TypeScript + Effect, so
`LoomLanguage` wraps `volar-service-typescript` and takes the `TypescriptSdk` the host
provides. It is always active: every `.loom` has a Frame.

The Frame's `languageId` is `loom`, distinct from a product section's `typescript`, so
the frame and the product section route to their own services even though they share
the tooling. `LoomLanguage` declares no `extensions` — the Frame is the substrate,
never a tangle target.

`volar-service-typescript` checks the Frame for composition correctness, but it speaks
TypeScript, not Loom. It cannot report a malformed tag or an anchor that names no
section — those are Loom's own health, and `loomDiagnostics` surfaces them.

The diagnostics arrive in `.loom` coordinates, so they belong to the source itself.
Volar dispatches a diagnostic request to each of a file's projected documents, and
`loomDiagnostics` answers only for the root — the whole-file mirror whose map to the
source is the identity. It returns nothing for the frame and the product documents,
whose generated spans are not where a source diagnostic lives. For the root it reads
the file path from the embedded uri, asks `FrameQuery` for that file's health, and
turns each Loom diagnostic into Volar's shape. A Loom severity becomes its editor code
— `error` is 1, `warning` is 2, `info` is 3 — and a diagnostic's source offsets become
a range through the document's own `positionAt`, which lands the squiggle on the exact
span the parser flagged.

`volar-service-typescript` navigates the frame as TypeScript, jumping between the
generated `Context.Service` classes. It cannot follow an anchor to the section it names,
nor find the anchors that compose a section — those are Loom's edges, not TypeScript's.
Two plugins answer them, each over the root mirror alone, the whole-file document whose
map to the source is the identity, so a position there is a source offset.

`loomDefinition` reads the symbol under the cursor and jumps to the definition its
name resolves to. It asks `FrameQuery` for two spans: the target through
`definition`, and the whole symbol under the cursor through `navigationRange`.
`toLocationLink` pairs them — the definition as the link's target, the symbol as its
`originSelectionRange` — so the editor underlines the whole name, an `::[…]` anchor
or a `Part I` alike, not the single word beneath the cursor.

`loomReferences` reads a heading or an anchor under the cursor and lists every place its
section is named — the heading itself and each anchor that composes it. It returns a
`Location` per result, the file keyed by its uri.

`volar-service-typescript` renames a TypeScript symbol; it does not know that a Loom
section's name lives in its heading and in every anchor that composes it. `loomRename`
renames the section as one symbol. `provideRenameRange` gives the editor the span its
rename box covers — the title or anchor name under the cursor — so the box wraps the
whole name. `provideRenameEdits` returns the edit: it asks `FrameQuery` for every span
the rename touches, and `groupEdits` turns them into a workspace edit, each span set to
the new name and keyed by its file's uri.

`volar-service-typescript` colours the frame as TypeScript; it has no colour for a
heading, an anchor, or a warp, the tokens Loom's own grammar draws. `loomSemanticTokens`
gives them theirs. Its legend names the standard token types Loom draws on — `namespace`,
`variable`, `keyword`, `string`, `operator` — so the user's theme colours them with no
extra setup, and the order follows `LoomSymbol`'s own, so a token's kind indexes straight
into it. `provideDocumentSemanticTokens` answers for the root alone, asks `FrameQuery`
for the file's coloured tokens, and turns each into Volar's tuple of line, character,
length, type, and modifiers — the type an index into the legend, and no modifiers.

`withoutHighlights` wraps each `volar-service-typescript` plugin to drop one feature:
document highlight. The Frame is the de dicto — the generated composition, and the prose
woven into it — so TypeScript's highlight there marks a generated span, and over the
woven prose it marks the whole section, which reads as the prose block being selected
when the cursor merely rests in it. A product section's own TypeScript service highlights
the code the author wrote; the Frame withholds the feature, returning no highlight rather
than the wrong one.

`LoomLanguage` composes its plugin sources: the wrapped `volar-service-typescript`
plugins that check the Frame, and over the `FrameQuery` the host provides,
`loomDiagnostics` for Loom's own health, `loomDefinition` and `loomReferences` for its
navigation, `loomRename` for renaming a section, and `loomSemanticTokens` for colouring
its tokens. Volar registers them all under the `loom` id.

`volar-service-typescript` supplies the Frame's TypeScript plugins. From Volar's
language service come the `LanguageServicePlugin` contract, the `LocationLink` a
definition returns, the `TextEdit` a rename produces, the `SemanticToken` tuple a colourer returns, and the editor's own `Diagnostic`
type, aliased `LspDiagnostic` to keep it apart from Loom's. Effect supplies `Array`, `pipe`, and the `Effect` the service
builds in. `@athrio/loom-ast` types a Loom `Diagnostic` and names the token colours in `SemanticTokenSchema`, `vscode-uri` parses an embedded
document's uri, and the `LanguageService` module gives `defineLanguageService` with the
`TypescriptSdk` and `FrameQuery` capabilities and the `FrameLocation` navigation answers
in.

blocks.markdown:
The Loom language server is an Effect program. `startLanguageServer` runs it on the Bun
runtime: it builds the pipeline layers once and captures a warm runtime — Volar's hooks are
synchronous, so the language plugin runs the projection on it. On `initialize` it loads the
editor's TypeScript, collects the active languages' Volar plugins, and registers them
alongside the language plugin. Two callers start it — a thin entry an editor loads, and the
`loom lsp` command — so the server lives in the CLI, the one tool every Loom user already has.

The server is built from Volar's language-server toolkit — the connection, the
project factory, and `loadTsdkByPath` — over a `ManagedRuntime`. It imports `LoomCompiler`,
the projector and cache the runtime warms, with the `DocumentSource`, `PackageConfig`, and
`LoomConfig` it requires, and the two halves this module registers, `loomLanguagePlugin` and
`loomServicePlugins`.

blocks.code:
import { Layer, ManagedRuntime } from 'effect'
import {
  createConnection,
  createServer,
  createTypeScriptProject,
  loadTsdkByPath,
} from '@volar/language-server/node'
import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'
import { LoomCompiler, DocumentSource } from './LoomCompiler'
import { PackageConfig } from './PackageConfig'
import { LoomConfig } from '@athrio/loom-config/LoomConfig'
import { loomLanguagePlugin, loomServicePlugins } from './LoomLanguagePlugin'

blocks.markdown:
A `ManagedRuntime`, built once from the pipeline layers, warms the services the server runs; the language plugin
runs the per-keystroke projection on it, and resolves each file's activated languages from
`LoomConfig` on it too. The `loadTsdkByPath` call loads the editor's own
TypeScript from the path the client sends, so a product section checks against the user's
standard library. Collecting service plugins requires reading the workspace configuration, so
the `onInitialize` handler hands the workspace folder to `loomServicePlugins` and awaits that Effect —
which finds the `.loom/` store and its `config.yaml` by walking up from the folder — before it registers the
language plugin and the collected plugins. On `initialized`, the server watches every `tsconfig.json` and
`jsconfig.json`, so editing a package's compiler options disposes its project and the next
request rebuilds it — the consumer's options take effect without an editor reload.

The server module assembles the imports and the program and exports `startLanguageServer`. It runs nothing on import — a caller decides when to start the server, so the same module serves both an editor that spawns it and the `loom lsp` command that calls it in-process.
