blocks.markdown:
This module is Loom's adapter to Volar, the editor toolkit the Loom language server is built on. It hands Volar two things for every `.loom` file: a language plugin and a set of service plugins.

The language plugin projects a `.loom` file to a virtual-code tree — the source itself as the de dicto root, where Loom serves its own diagnostics and navigation, and the de re code sections beneath it, each embedded in its own language. Volar maps every diagnostic, hover, and definition back to where the author wrote it.

The service plugins are the editor features themselves: diagnostics, hover, completion. The server resolves the project's active languages, runs each one's `plugins` Effect, and flattens the results into the single list Volar registers.

`loomLanguagePlugin` is the `LanguagePlugin` Volar asks for. It claims every `.loom` path and projects the file to a virtual-code tree. The de dicto root is the source itself, which Loom's own service checks and navigates; the product sections are embedded codes, which each language service checks against a program of its own. It projects through `LoomCompiler`, whose `compile` verb runs the file's composition — discovering the corpus and inlining cross-file `::[…]` transclusions — so the editor resolves a corpus the way the tangler does. `projectIsolated` runs that projection, then marks each composition root as its own module.

The file's text comes through a passive `Source`. `editorSource` reads the open file from the snapshot Volar hands over and each sibling from disk, and lists the corpus directory through `loomsUnder` so the projector discovers the file's corpus. It never calls back into Volar, so running the composition cannot re-enter the projector.

After projecting, the plugin tells Volar what the file depends on, and Volar re-projects the file when any dependency changes. `associate` reads the corpus the projection reached and calls `ctx.getAssociatedScript` for every module the file transitively imports. The registration runs on the warm corpus, after the build, so the projection it triggers is a cache hit rather than a re-entrant build. It registers the whole transitive reach, not the direct imports alone, because Volar marks a dependent dirty one hop at a time on a snapshot change — so a change deep in a chain reaches every file that transcludes it only when each has declared the whole chain.

Volar's hooks are synchronous, so the projection runs through `Runtime.runSync` on the warm runtime built at startup. When a file's own text changes, `updateVirtualCode` evicts its build through `change`, then reprojects and re-registers. A reprojection Volar triggered because a dependency changed leaves the build cache alone — the file's own text is unchanged — and the changed dependency, evicted by its own update, rebuilds when the composition runs over it.

A product language service holds each composition root in a program of its own. A root with no import or export of its own is a global script, and two such roots would clash on a shared top-level name once a program holds them both. `isolateRoots` walks the projected tree and appends `moduleMarker`, an empty `export {}`, to every TypeScript-family root, turning each into a module with its own scope. `tsLanguages` names the TypeScript-family languages the marker applies to — every other language isolates itself or has no notion of a module. The marker carries no source mapping, so it stays invisible, and the plugin appends it once at projection, so a root's snapshot stays stable until its own source changes and Volar never re-parses it on a keystroke elsewhere.

blocks.code:
const tsLanguages: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set([
  'typescript',
  'tsx',
  'javascript',
  'jsx',
])

const moduleMarker = '\nexport {}\n'

const isolateRoots = (code: VirtualCode): VirtualCode => ({
  ...code,
  snapshot: tsLanguages.has(code.languageId)
    ? stringSnapshot(
        code.snapshot.getText(0, code.snapshot.getLength()) + moduleMarker,
      )
    : code.snapshot,
  embeddedCodes: Array.map(code.embeddedCodes ?? [], (child) =>
    isolateRoots(child),
  ),
})

blocks.markdown:
`loomServicePlugins` is the collect-and-register pass. Before it loads anything, it installs the host runtime on the global — Effect, the service contract, and the editor's TypeScript compiler — so a loaded service reads the host's instances rather than bundling its own and missing the capabilities the host provides. It then reads the project's active languages, loads each through `loadActive` — `LoomLanguage` always, and a `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` package for every other id, imported from the Loom store — then indexes them in the `LanguageRegistry` and runs each service's `plugins` Effect with the host's capabilities. A language activated but not installed in the store drops with a warning, and the rest load on. It provides three capabilities. The `TypescriptSdk` is the editor's own compiler, so analysis matches the user's project. A `FrameQuery`, built over the warm corpus by `frameQueryOver`, lets the frame service read a file's Loom diagnostics. A `Composition`, built by `compositionOver`, hands a product service the corpus as a filesystem — every root as the code it composes, at the path it composes to — so its analyzer resolves a cross-file import against live composition rather than the output on disk. The editor still hands each service its Products directly, a resolved root with its members folded in; `Composition` adds only the rest of the corpus. Each service also gets its slice of the workspace's `settings`, keyed by language id, so it tunes itself from the configuration. A service that fails to build — an external binary that will not start — drops to no plugins rather than taking the others down. The pass dedupes by plugin name, so a plugin two services happen to share registers with Volar once.

`frameQueryOver` builds the one capability from the runtime. Each reader composes a file's corpus and answers from it: `diagnostics` returns the open module's Loom health, `definition` follows an anchor to the section it names, `references` finds every anchor that names the section under the cursor, `rename` and `renameRange` give the spans a rename rewrites and the one the rename box covers, and `semanticTokens` gives each of the file's tokens its colour. They read synchronously, on the same warm runtime the projection uses, and close over it, so the frame service can call them long after this pass, when the editor asks on a keystroke. `compositionOver` builds the `Composition` reader the same way: `rootsFor` composes a file's corpus and returns every root as a `ComposedFile`, on that same warm runtime.

The plugin draws on Volar for its contracts — `LanguagePlugin` and `LanguageServicePlugin` — and its `CodegenContext` and `VirtualCode`, which the per-call source and the cache check read. Effect captures the runtime and runs the collection pass. From the pipeline it takes `LoomCompiler`, which projects and caches the corpus, and the `Source` seam with its `ReadError`. From `@athrio/loom-lang-services` it takes the `LanguageService` contract, the `FrameQuery` capability the collect pass provides, the `LanguageRegistry` that indexes the active set, and `loadActive`, which resolves a project's active services — `LoomLanguage` and each installed `@athrio/loom-service-<id>` package. `installHostRuntime` lends those loaded services the host's runtime. From `@athrio/loom-config` it reads the workspace's active languages out of the compiled manifest. `URI` keys a script for `getAssociatedScript`, and Bun's `fs` reads an unopened import.

blocks.markdown: `LoomVirtualCode` is the terminal model of the pipeline — the projection a `.loom` presents to Volar. It is plain data, not an AST: a tree of nodes, each holding a string of `code` and a list of `Mapping`s back to the source. `LoomVirtualCodeBuilder` builds it, and `LoomCompiler`'s `toVolar` adapts it to Volar's runtime types at the editor edge.

A `Mapping` pairs a generated span — `genStart` and `genLength`, offsets into this node's `code` — with the `.loom` `source` span it projects from. Its `kind` chooses which editor features Volar forwards at that span. Most kinds are the symbol kinds `LoomSymbol` names — a heading title, an anchor, a warp — so a mapping over an author's token forwards exactly the features that kind's profile allows. Two kinds stand outside that set, for the projection's own structure. A `source` span is the root's whole-file identity mapping, the one Loom's own diagnostics ride back to the editor. A `product` span is a stretch of resolved de re code.

blocks.code:
export const MappingKindSchema = Schema.Union([
  SymbolKindSchema,
  Schema.Literal('source'),
  Schema.Literal('product'),
])
export type MappingKind = typeof MappingKindSchema.Type

export const MappingSchema = Schema.Struct({
  genStart: Schema.Number,
  genLength: Schema.Number,
  source: PositionSchema,
  kind: Schema.optional(MappingKindSchema),
})
export type Mapping = typeof MappingSchema.Type

blocks.markdown: A `LoomVirtualCode` is one node of the tree: an `id`, a `languageId`, its `code`, its `mappings`, and its `embeddedCodes` children. It mirrors Volar's own `VirtualCode` shape but stays plain data — `code` as a string, our `Mapping` rather than Volar's. The interface is declared before the schema so the schema can recurse through `embeddedCodes` with `Schema.suspend`.
Effect's `Schema` defines the model. `Position` from the foundation types each mapping's source span, and `SymbolKind` from `LoomSymbol` gives a symbol mapping its kind.

blocks.markdown:
`ProseLanguage` gives a `.loom`'s prose its editor support. Prose is Markdown, so this service extends `volar-service-markdown` — headings, links, lists, fenced-code highlighting, and formatting — and registers it as a language of Loom's own. A `{Prose}` section, and the prose of every other section, projects to a `prose` document the editor reads through this service.

Prose carries Loom's anchors too, the `::[…]` links between sections. Their navigation already rides the root mirror, where `LoomLanguage` answers go-to-definition and references over the whole file. So `ProseLanguage` adds the Markdown layer beneath them, and the two compose in the editor: Markdown for the prose, Loom for the anchors.

The prose document's `languageId` is `prose`, distinct from a real `.md` file, so a `.loom`'s prose routes here and nothing else claims it. `create` from `volar-service-markdown` takes a document selector, and pointing it at `prose` retargets the Markdown service onto Loom's prose without forking it.

`ProseLanguage` loads `volar-service-markdown` on demand and wraps it under the `prose` selector. The Markdown service is a heavy dependency the editor needs only once a `.loom` opens its prose, so the import is dynamic — deferred to the first call rather than paid at startup. A load that fails leaves the service with no plugins, degrading prose to plain text while every other language serves on. It needs no host capability: the Markdown service reads the document on its own, and Loom's own answers come from the root.

Effect runs the on-demand load and builds the `plugins` result; `volar-service-markdown` is imported dynamically inside it rather than at the module head. The `LanguageService` module gives `defineLanguageService`, the contract this service satisfies.

blocks.markdown:
A cursor in a `.loom` file rests on one of a fixed set of things: a heading title, an
anchor, a warp, a language specifier, a sink, an arrow, a tilde, or prose. Each is a
**symbol kind**, and each kind supports a fixed set of editor features — how it
colors, which requests the editor may make on it, and what its navigation resolves
to. `LoomSymbol` holds that answer in one place, so a token's editor behavior is a
table entry, not logic scattered across the mappings, the navigation, and the
highlighter.

This mirrors how Volar already models the question. Volar's `CodeInformation` marks,
per source span, which language features that span forwards — `navigation`,
`semantic`, `structure`, `verification`, `completion`. `LoomSymbol` names the same
flags in `SpanFeatures`, and adds the two things Volar leaves to the plugin: how the
token colors, and what its navigation means. One profile per kind; three consumers
read it — the frame's mappings, its navigation, and its semantic tokens — so their
behavior cannot drift apart.

A `SymbolKind` names one class of token. A section's `headingTitle` is the name
anchors resolve to. A `sectionAnchor` is a `::[Title]` reference to a section; a
`warpAnchor` is a `::[name]` reference to a value warp declared in the same section.
A `warpDef` is a `{{name = value}}` declaration. A `specifier` is a heading's
`{Language}`, a `sink` its `[file]`. An `arrow` opens a code block and a
`tilde` closes it. A `frontmatterTitle` is a chapter's name, the identity a
table-of-contents entry resolves to. A `frontmatterPart` is the `Part N` a
chapter belongs to — a reference to the part — and a `frontmatterPartName` is
the part's name, announced once in the part's opening chapter. The chapter
number is a plain `frontmatterMembership`, and a `frontmatterValue` is the
`Package` or `Language` a field binds. In the table of contents, a `tocPart` is
a part's heading, which navigates to where the part is announced, and a
`tocEntry` is a chapter's line, which navigates to the chapter it names.
Everything else a person reads is `prose`.

blocks.code:
export const SymbolKindSchema = Schema.Literals(['headingTitle', 'sectionAnchor', 'warpAnchor', 'warpDef', 'specifier', 'sink', 'arrow', 'tilde', 'prose', 'frontmatterMembership', 'frontmatterValue', 'frontmatterTitle', 'frontmatterPart', 'frontmatterPartName', 'tocPart', 'tocEntry'])
export type SymbolKind = typeof SymbolKindSchema.Type

blocks.markdown:
A `SymbolProfile` is what the editor may do with a kind. `SpanFeatures` carries
Volar's own per-span flags, so the frame's mapping for a token forwards exactly the
requests the kind supports and no others. `semantic` names the token's colour, drawn
from a small set of the editor's standard semantic-token types. A kind that carries
no colour of its own — prose, rendered as Markdown — leaves it `None`.

`profileOf` is the table. Each kind maps to its profile through one exhaustive
`Match`, so a new kind fails to compile until it is profiled, and adding a token to
Loom is one entry here rather than a change in every consumer.

A `Symbol` is one token in a source: its kind and the span it occupies. The frame
reads a document's symbols to lay its mappings and its colours, and finds the one
under a cursor to answer navigation.

`symbolsOf` reads every symbol a document declares, in one pass. Every warp is a
`warpDef`. An anchor is a `warpAnchor` when its name matches a warp in scope — the
warps its section and the document preamble declare — and a `sectionAnchor`
otherwise, since only a warp resolves within the section while a title resolves
across the corpus. A heading contributes its title, specifier, and sink; a code
block its arrow or tilde. The frontmatter contributes its title, its part, and
its field values, and a `{TOC}` section its parts and chapter entries.
`symbolAt` finds the symbol a cursor rests in, so navigation asks the table which
kind it is before resolving.

From Effect come the composition combinators, `Match` for the exhaustive table and
`Option` and `Array` for the classification pass. From the AST come the document,
section, and frontmatter shapes the pass walks, and the token shapes it reads — the
warp, the anchor, the heading's parts, and a toc weft. `Position` is a source span.
